proposals.title: High-Throughput, Ultra-High-Resolution Measurement of Protein Turnover Dynamics Across Yeast Strains Using Dynamic SILAC and SLIM-Based Ion Mobility-Orbitrap Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, understanding proteome dynamics—specifically protein synthesis and degradation rates—is crucial for deciphering cellular adaptation, stress responses, and the molecular basis of phenotypic variation across genetic backgrounds. Current methods for measuring protein turnover, like dynamic SILAC, are limited in throughput and molecular resolution when applied to complex multi-strain comparisons, hindering systems-level analysis of proteostatic networks.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating dynamic Stable Isotope Labeling by Amino acids in Cell culture (SILAC) with the high-resolution separation power of Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) and ultra-high mass accuracy of Orbitrap/FTICR platforms will enable the precise, parallel quantification of protein turnover rates across hundreds of proteins in multiple Saccharomyces cerevisiae strains, revealing conserved and strain-specific regulatory nodes in protein homeostasis.
proposals.method: We will employ a pulsed dynamic SILAC protocol across a panel of yeast strains, followed by lysis and tryptic digestion. Peptides will be analyzed using a dual-gated SLIM-ion mobility module coupled to an Orbitrap mass spectrometer, as developed in the researcher's prior work. This platform provides ultra-high-resolution ion mobility separation prior to MS analysis, reducing spectral complexity and enabling accurate quantification of light and heavy peptide pairs. Data will be processed using MaxQuant for SILAC-based quantification, with custom algorithms to model incorporation kinetics and calculate synthesis (ks) and degradation (kd) rates for each protein in each strain.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first comprehensive, strain-resolved atlas of protein turnover in a model eukaryote, directly linking genetic variation to proteome dynamics. It will establish a novel high-throughput analytical pipeline combining metabolic labeling with advanced ion mobility-MS, setting a new standard for precision in measuring protein kinetics. The data will provide a foundational resource for modeling metabolic and regulatory networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct coupling of dynamic SILAC for kinetic measurements with the exceptional separation capabilities of SLIM-ion mobility to deconvolute complex peptide mixtures from multiple strains in a single, high-resolution analysis, moving beyond static abundance measurements to comparative dynamics.

proposals.title: Proteome-Initiated Feedback Loops: A High-Resolution SLIM-IMS-Orbitrap MS and Single-Cell Transcriptomic Atlas of Protein-to-mRNA Regulation
proposals.motivation: While transcript-to-protein regulation is well-studied, the inverse feedback—where protein levels or activity control transcript abundance—remains a critical but poorly mapped layer of cellular regulation. This protein-initiated feedback is central to homeostasis, signaling, and disease states like cancer, yet comprehensive, system-wide studies linking specific protein states to transcriptomic outcomes are lacking. Pre-2026 proteogenomic studies, including Dr. Smith's ovarian cancer work, have established correlative maps but not causative, dynamic protein-to-transcript relationships.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, quantifiable protein states—including abundance, complex formation, and post-translational modifications—act as dominant initiators of rapid, coherent transcriptomic reprogramming, and that these regulatory circuits can be systematically decoded by coupling ultra-high-resolution protein measurements with single-cell transcriptomics.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual-pronged, dynamic perturbation approach. First, we will use the Dual-Gated SLIM-IMS-Orbitrap platform for ultra-high-resolution, multiplexed analysis of protein complexes and modifications over time following targeted perturbations (e.g., kinase inhibition, ligand-induced degradation). Second, we will perform parallel, single-cell RNA-seq (using 10x Genomics or similar pre-2026 technology) on aliquots from the same cell populations. Data integration will use a custom computational pipeline based on Gaussian Process regression and causal inference algorithms (like PCMCI) to model time-lagged relationships, identifying proteins whose state changes predict subsequent transcript abundance shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first causally-annotated, high-resolution atlas of protein-to-transcript regulatory networks in a human cancer cell model. It will move the field beyond correlation to mechanism, identifying key protein 'master regulators' of transcription. The integrated SLIM-IMS-MS and transcriptomic methodology will establish a new standard for dynamic proteogenomic studies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly inverting the central dogma of proteogenomics—treating the proteome as the independent variable—and in the technical integration of lossless ion manipulation for protein complex analysis with single-cell transcriptomics to resolve these dynamics with unprecedented precision and causal inference.

proposals.title: A Multi-Omics Predictive Model for Tissue-Specific Protein Abundance Using Integrated SLIM-IMS-MS Proteomic Validation
proposals.motivation: While transcriptomics and genomics are foundational for systems biology, the functional cellular state is defined by proteins. Current models predicting protein abundance from transcript and genotype data have limited accuracy, especially for post-translationally modified or low-abundance proteins, creating a critical gap in linking genotype to phenotype. High-fidelity protein-level predictions are essential for interpreting disease-associated genetic variants and understanding cancer proteogenomics, as highlighted in studies of high-grade serous ovarian cancer.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating transcript levels, cis- and trans-acting genetic variants, and prior protein-protein interaction network data within an ensemble machine learning framework can accurately predict tissue-specific protein abundance, and these predictions can be rigorously validated and refined using ultra-high-resolution ion mobility-mass spectrometry (IMS-MS) data.
proposals.method: We will build an ensemble model (XGBoost and deep neural networks) trained on paired transcriptomic (RNA-seq), genomic (SNP array/WGS), and high-quality proteomic data from sources like CPTAC. The proteomic ground truth will be generated using a novel dual-gated Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM)-IMS-Orbitrap platform, providing ultra-high-resolution mobility and mass measurements for precise protein identification and quantification. Feature engineering will include cis-pQTLs, predicted protein stability from sequence, and network proximity scores. Model performance will be assessed by cross-validation and by its ability to predict protein levels in an independent cohort analyzed via the same SLIM-IMS-MS pipeline.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, validated computational model for protein-level prediction, directly addressing the 'missing link' in multi-omics integration. It will provide a public resource of predicted protein abundances for tissues with limited proteomic coverage. Furthermore, it will demonstrate a novel closed-loop workflow where state-of-the-art, high-resolution IMS-MS data both trains and critically tests a predictive biological model.
proposals.novelty: The integration of ultra-high-resolution SLIM-IMS-MS data as the core proteomic validation layer for a predictive model is novel, moving beyond standard LC-MS/MS. This approach uniquely leverages Dr. Smith's expertise in advanced ion manipulation and high-resolution mass analysis to solve a central problem in computational proteogenomics.

proposals.title: Direct, High-Resolution Analysis of Complex Native Protein Mixtures via Integrated High-Field Asymmetric Waveform Ion Mobility Spectrometry and Charge Detection Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: Current top-down proteomics relies heavily on liquid chromatography (LC) or capillary electrophoresis (CE) separations prior to mass spectrometry, introducing analysis time, potential sample losses, and complexity. For rapid, direct characterization of complex biological systems like intact protein complexes or cellular lysates, a separation-free MS platform would be transformative. This addresses a core challenge in Richard D. Smith's research on integrated 'slim' (Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations) and high-resolution MS for biomolecular analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: That the ultra-high resolution and extended ion manipulation capabilities of a dual-gated SLIM-FTICR platform, combined with charge determination via individual ion measurements, can sufficiently resolve and characterize proteins from highly complex mixtures without prior chromatographic or electrophoretic separation.
proposals.method: We will employ a direct infusion ESI source coupled to a high-performance SLIM module for extended path length, ultra-high-resolution ion mobility separations based on collisional cross-section. Ions will then be analyzed by FTICR mass spectrometry, implementing individual ion charge detection (as pioneered in Smith's 1990s work) via image current analysis for precise m/z determination of co-transmitted species. Data processing will use custom algorithms for deconvoluting overlapping mobility and m/z features, leveraging the orthogonal dispersion in CCS and mass dimensions to assign proteins from known databases.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a foundational proof-of-concept for direct, high-resolution analysis of intact protein mixtures, significantly accelerating sample throughput. It will establish a new benchmark for the resolving power required to bypass LC/CE in top-down proteomics and provide a framework for real-time monitoring of complex protein assemblies and modifications.
proposals.novelty: While LC/CE-MS is standard, directly challenging its necessity by combining ultra-high-resolution SLIM-based ion mobility with individual ion FTICR charge detection for complex mixtures is unprecedented. This integrates Smith's historic expertise in charge measurement with his group's most advanced ion manipulation technology for a radical simplification of the workflow.

proposals.title: Ultra-High Mass Analysis of Synthetic and Genomic Oligonucleotides via High-Resolution Charge Detection Mass Spectrometry and SLIM Super-Resolution Ion Mobility
proposals.motivation: The therapeutic and diagnostic use of large oligonucleotides (e.g., mRNA, long antisense, CRISPR guides) is expanding rapidly, but robust, direct mass measurement for species >100 kDa remains a critical analytical gap. Current ESI-MS methods, effective for megadalton proteins and complexes, falter with oligonucleotides due to their lower charge states, dense charge spacing, and susceptibility to adduct formation, preventing accurate mass determination for validation and characterization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating ultra-high-resolution Fourier transform ion cyclotron resonance (FTICR) mass spectrometry with Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) super-long-path ion mobility and charge detection will enable the resolution of closely spaced charge states and accurate mass determination of single ions for oligonucleotides exceeding 100,000 Da.
proposals.method: We will adapt a dual-gated SLIM-Orbitrap/FTICR platform, leveraging Dr. Smith's expertise in SLIM and high-field FTICR. Large, low-charge oligonucleotide ions will be generated using gentle nano-electrospray ionization (nano-ESI) with supercharging additives. Ions will be separated and focused using extended-path SLIM mobility to reduce chemical noise. Individual ion charge will be determined via image current measurement in a modified detection cell, followed by high-resolution m/z analysis in the FTICR, enabling mass calculation (mass = m/z * charge - charge*mass(H+)). Data processing will utilize algorithms from 'Reactions for Charge Determination' (RCD) MS to deconvolve overlapping charge envelopes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational ESI-MS workflow for the precise (<0.01% error) mass measurement of therapeutic oligonucleotides and genomic fragments beyond 100 kDa. It will extend the 'weighing' capability of native MS from the proteomic to the genomic domain, providing a critical tool for quality control in nucleic acid therapeutics and for studying non-covalent nucleic acid-protein complexes.
proposals.novelty: While SLIM and FTICR have been used for proteins, their combined application with single-ion charge detection is novel for oligonucleotides. This approach directly addresses the core challenge of low, unresolved charge states by physically separating ions by mobility and directly measuring charge, rather than relying solely on spectral deconvolution.

proposals.title: Enhanced Detection of High-Mass Ions via Synchronized Ion Polarization and Frequency-Domain Signal Processing in FT-ICR and Orbitrap Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: The analysis of high molecular weight (HMW) proteins and complexes by high-resolution mass spectrometry is critical for structural biology and proteomics, but detection sensitivity decreases sharply with increasing m/z due to reduced charge density and image current signal. Pre-2026 platforms like SLIM-enabled ultra-high-resolution IMS-Orbitrap and FT-ICR systems excel at separation but are fundamentally limited by the weak signals from massive, low-charge ions, hindering the study of intact macromolecular assemblies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sensitivity for detecting HMW ions in FT-based mass analyzers can be significantly improved by actively manipulating ion polarization states prior to detection and applying tailored frequency-domain signal processing algorithms to the induced image current.
proposals.method: We will integrate a novel, in-vacuum ion polarization chamber post-mobility separation (using SLIM) but prior to injection into an FT-ICR cell or Orbitrap. This chamber will use precisely timed, asymmetric electric fields to align the dipole moments of large biomolecular ions, enhancing their coherent motion. Concurrently, we will develop a matched filtering algorithm based on a modified wavelet transform, optimized for the expected transient decay profiles and harmonic complexity of polarized HMW ions, to extract signal from noise more efficiently than standard Fourier transform methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a combined hardware and software framework that directly addresses the core physical limitation of HMW ion detection in FT-MS. It will provide a measurable increase in signal-to-noise ratio for ions >50 kDa, enabling more confident identification and characterization of low-abundance protein complexes and post-translational modifications on large proteins.
proposals.novelty: While ion manipulation for mobility separation is established, the active control of ion polarization specifically to boost image current signal is a novel concept. Furthermore, moving beyond generic apodization to dedicated wavelet-based processing for the transient signals of polarized HMW ions represents an unexplored algorithmic approach in MS detection.

proposals.title: Enhanced Collision-Induced Dissociation via Ion Polarization and SLIM-Guided Energy Tailoring for Top-Down Structural Analysis of Megadalton Protein Complexes
proposals.motivation: Current tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS) and collision-induced dissociation (CID) methods are optimized for peptides and small proteins, but yield limited, low-information fragment patterns for very large proteins and non-covalent complexes above ~100 kDa. This gap hinders the direct interrogation of intact macromolecular assembly structure and dynamics, which is critical for understanding diseases like high-grade serous ovarian cancer where altered protein complexes drive pathology. Extending high-information CID to the megadalton range would revolutionize structural proteomics.
proposals.hypothesis: By precisely controlling the rotational and translational energy deposition of large, high-charge ions during CID through pre-collision ion polarization and structures for lossless ion manipulations (SLIM)-guided energy tailoring, we can generate more informative, sequence-rich fragment ions from intact protein complexes exceeding 1 MDa.
proposals.method: We will integrate a high-field ion polarization chamber upstream of a SLIM-based ion mobility device coupled to an Orbitrap Eclipse Tribrid mass spectrometer. Large protein complexes (e.g., ribosomes, viral capsids) will be introduced via native electrospray. The polarization chamber will align ion dipoles prior to entry into a novel, elongated SLIM collision cell where a stepped, mobility-resolved DC gradient will be applied. This gradient will be algorithmically optimized in real-time using a feedback loop from the FTICR detector, varying collision energy based on the ion's mobility (a proxy for cross-section) and charge state derived from individual ion FTICR measurements, to maximize fragmentation efficiency while preserving non-covalent sub-complex information.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new CID methodology capable of producing interpretable fragmentation spectra from intact megadalton assemblies, moving top-down proteomics into the realm of native structural biology. It will provide a complementary technique to native IM-MS by adding residue-level structural constraints on complex subunits. The developed platform will enable direct correlation of complex stoichiometry, topology, and sequence variants in a single experiment.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the combined use of ion polarization to control collision orientation and SLIM-based mobility-resolved energy stepping, moving beyond uniform collision energy application. This approach explicitly addresses the fundamental challenges of energy redistribution and inefficient cleavage in very large, vibrationally complex ions, which is not tackled by existing high-mass CID or ETD methods.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput, Continuous-Flow ESI-SLIM-IMS-MS Platform for Real-Time Monitoring of Protein Expression and Quality in Industrial Bioprocesses
proposals.motivation: Industrial bioprocessing for therapeutic proteins requires precise, real-time monitoring of product titer and quality attributes (e.g., glycosylation, aggregation) to ensure yield and safety. Current analytical methods, like offline LC-MS, introduce significant delays (hours to days), preventing immediate process control. Adapting electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) for direct, continuous analysis of fermentation and purification streams could revolutionize bioprocess optimization and quality-by-design.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating advanced ion handling techniques with robust, automated fluidics, ESI-MS can be engineered for stable, high-throughput operation on complex, concentrated bioprocess streams, providing real-time molecular data without compromising instrument performance.
proposals.method: We will develop a continuous-flow sampling interface with automated dilution and desalting modules to condition concentrated fermentation broth for direct infusion. This will be coupled to a high-speed ESI source and a Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) ion mobility spectrometer, leveraging Dr. Smith's expertise in SLIM and high-resolution FTICR/Orbitrap platforms. The SLIM-IMS stage will provide rapid, gas-phase separation to reduce spectral complexity from matrix components. Data acquisition will use fast, targeted mass and mobility algorithms (e.g., selective ion monitoring with IMS filtering) on an Orbitrap mass analyzer, enabling near-real-time quantitation of target proteins and critical impurities.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a first-of-its-kind, validated prototype for continuous bioprocess monitoring, moving MS from a lab-based quality control tool to an inline process analytical technology (PAT). It will provide a foundational methodology for real-time, molecule-specific feedback control in industrial bioreactors, significantly shortening development cycles and improving product consistency.
proposals.novelty: While ESI-MS is a mature lab technique, its adaptation for stable, long-term, and automated analysis of raw, high-conductivity industrial process streams is a major unsolved engineering challenge. The novel integration of SLIM-based ion mobility as a rapid, inline separation step prior to high-resolution MS detection for this application is unprecedented.

proposals.title: High-Resolution Native Mass Spectrometry and Ion Mobility Analysis of Transient Protein-Ligand Complexes Using Polarized SLIM-Based Ultra-High-Field FTICR
proposals.motivation: Understanding the structural dynamics and stability of non-covalent protein complexes is fundamental to biology and drug discovery, yet many transient interactions remain poorly characterized due to their low abundance and lability. Pre-2026 native mass spectrometry (nMS) struggles with the trade-off between preserving fragile complexes and achieving the ultra-high mass resolution needed to resolve subtle stoichiometries and bound water/lipid adducts. This gap limits our insight into the initial, weak binding events that often precede high-affinity interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating gentle, polarized nano-electrospray ionization (nano-ESI) sources with ultra-high-resolution Fourier-transform ion cyclotron resonance (FTICR) mass analysis and high-resolution ion mobility separation via Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) will enable the direct detection, mass measurement, and collision cross-section determination of low-population, transient non-covalent complexes that are currently inaccessible.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual-gated SLIM-IMS-Orbitrap/FTICR platform, modified with a polarized ESI source operating at sub-atmospheric pressure and minimal in-source activation. Complexes will be generated from purified proteins and ligands in volatile, native-like buffers. IMS separation will be performed in the SLIM device under low-field conditions to preserve complexes, followed by ultra-high-resolution FTICR mass analysis (as pioneered in the Smith lab) for precise mass determination. Stability will be probed by incrementally increasing collisional activation in the SLIM traps or transfer regions, monitoring dissociation thresholds via FTICR.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust methodological framework for the integrated mass and mobility analysis of labile biomolecular complexes, directly contributing to the 'Integrated Proteogenomic' characterization paradigm by adding a native complexomics dimension. It will provide unprecedented detail on the stoichiometry, stability, and structural compactness of early-stage drug-target and protein-protein interactions, informing rational design in chemical biology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific synergy of polarized, ultra-gentle ESI, high-resolution SLIM-based ion mobility for intact complex separation, and ultra-high-mass-accuracy FTICR—a combination not yet fully realized pre-2026—to target specifically the most fragile, transient complexes rather than stable ones.

proposals.title: Deconvolution of Protein Conformational Dynamics via High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Orbitrap Mass Spectrometry and Inverse Modeling of ESI Charge State Distributions
proposals.motivation: Understanding protein folding and misfolding is central to biology and disease, yet real-time monitoring of conformational dynamics in solution remains challenging. Electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) charge state distributions (CSDs) encode conformational information, but current analyses are largely qualitative. A robust, quantitative inverse model to extract folding landscapes from CSDs would transform ESI-MS into a powerful tool for dynamic structural biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the time-evolving ESI-MS charge state distribution of a protein under controlled perturbation can be deconvoluted using an inverse modeling framework to reconstruct quantitative populations of discrete conformational states and their interconversion kinetics.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual-gated Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM)-Ion Mobility-Orbitrap MS platform for ultra-high-resolution separation and mass analysis. Proteins will be subjected to controlled in-solution perturbations (e.g., pH jump, rapid dilution from denaturant) via a microfluidic mixing device coupled directly to ESI. The inverse problem will be solved using a maximum entropy deconvolution algorithm, constrained by ion mobility-derived collision cross-sections (CCS) as prior structural information, to map CSDs onto discrete conformational ensembles and extract kinetic parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel computational-experimental pipeline for quantifying protein folding dynamics directly from native MS data. It will provide a generalizable method to extract free energy landscapes and kinetic rates for conformational transitions, advancing fundamental studies of protein stability, allostery, and misfolding mechanisms relevant to diseases like cancer and neurodegeneration.
proposals.novelty: The integration of high-resolution ion mobility-Orbitrap data with a formal inverse problem-solving approach to deconvolute CSDs into quantitative kinetic models represents a significant advance beyond the current state-of-the-art of qualitative or semi-quantitative correlation.

proposals.title: Routine Integration of Collision Cross-Section (CCS) as a Primary Molecular Descriptor in Untargeted Omics via AutoCCS-Enabled Workflows
proposals.motivation: In untargeted metabolomics and proteomics, confident annotation of molecular features remains a critical bottleneck. While high-resolution mass spectrometry provides accurate mass, orthogonal data like ion mobility-derived collision cross-section (CCS) significantly improves identification confidence. However, CCS determination is not yet a routine, automated component of standard omics data processing pipelines, limiting its widespread utility for complex biological samples.
proposals.hypothesis: Embedding an automated CCS calculation and matching pipeline (AutoCCS) directly into established, high-throughput untargeted omics software will enable the routine use of CCS as a robust, orthogonal molecular descriptor, significantly improving annotation confidence and reducing false discovery rates in large-scale studies.
proposals.method: We will integrate the AutoCCS algorithm, developed for automated CCS extraction from SLIM-based or traditional drift tube ion mobility-mass spectrometry data, into the open-source MS-DIAL and/or MZmine 3 frameworks. The integration will involve creating a dedicated module that automatically processes ion mobility dimension data, calculates CCS values using the Mason-Schamp equation with appropriate calibration, and appends these values as a core metadata column alongside m/z and retention time. The module will include a CCS-aware feature alignment step and enable matching against pre-2026 experimental CCS libraries (e.g., McLean, Lipidyzer) and in-silico prediction databases within the software's native identification workflow.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a freely available, production-ready software extension that makes CCS a first-class citizen in untargeted omics analysis. It will provide the community with a standardized method to routinely acquire and utilize CCS data, directly enhancing molecular annotation rates in applications like cancer proteogenomics and cellular metabolomics. The integrated pipeline will set a new benchmark for data richness in public omics data repositories.
proposals.novelty: While standalone tools for CCS exist, the seamless, automated integration of a robust CCS pipeline into the most widely used untargeted omics software platforms for routine, high-throughput analysis is novel. This moves CCS from a specialized, post-hoc measurement to an intrinsic, parallel data dimension captured during initial processing.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Collision Cross-Section Profiling via Ultra-High Sensitivity SLIM-IMS-Orbitrap Mass Spectrometry and AutoCCS Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Single-cell proteomics is critical for understanding cellular heterogeneity in development and disease, but current mass spectrometry (MS) workflows primarily identify and quantify proteins. The structural dimension, represented by the Collision Cross-Section (CCS), is largely missing due to insufficient sensitivity and material requirements of traditional ion mobility spectrometry (IMS). Acquiring CCS values from single-cell lysates would provide a transformative, orthogonal descriptor of protein conformation and complex stoichiometry, unavailable from sequence alone.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating ultra-high-sensitivity, dual-gated Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) IMS with Orbitrap MS and adapting the AutoCCS algorithm for low-ion-count conditions, we can achieve robust, high-resolution CCS measurements from the limited ion populations (<100 amol per protein) generated from single mammalian cells.
proposals.method: We will construct a nano-electrospray ionization source coupled to a dual-gated SLIM super-long path IMS module and an ultra-high-field Orbitrap mass analyzer. Ions from single-cell lysates (prepared via nanoPOTS or similar) will be accumulated and cooled in an upstream ion funnel. The dual-gated SLIM will enable lossless trapping and extended path separation for high-resolution IMS. We will modify the AutoCCS algorithm, which uses calibrant-independent CCS determination from mobility peak shapes in FT-IMS, to operate with Bayesian inference and error modeling for sparse ion counts, enabling statistically valid CCS assignment from sub-1000 ion arrivals per feature.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first platform capable of routine, high-confidence CCS measurement for hundreds of proteins from individual cells. It will create a new data type—single-cell CCS maps—enabling studies of protein conformational changes and complex variations at cellular resolution. The adapted AutoCCS algorithm will become a key tool for ultra-sensitive structural proteomics.
proposals.novelty: While SLIM-IMS-MS has achieved remarkable resolution, its application to single-cell analysis for CCS determination is unexplored. The novelty lies in the co-development of the front-end ion handling for extreme sensitivity with the back-end algorithmic adaptation of AutoCCS to statistically handle the inherent ion scarcity of single-cell measurements, moving beyond identification to structural phenotyping.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Collision Cross-Section (CCS) Screening via an Optimized AutoCCS Pipeline Integrated with SLIM Ultra-High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Orbitrap MS
proposals.motivation: Large-scale drug discovery and untargeted metabolomics require high-confidence compound identification, where ion mobility-derived Collision Cross-Section (CCS) is a crucial orthogonal descriptor. Current CCS extraction workflows, like AutoCCS, are not optimized for the throughput and real-time analysis demands of screening thousands of samples. This bottleneck limits the utility of high-resolution ion mobility-mass spectrometry (IM-MS) platforms, such as Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) coupled to Orbitrap systems, in applied screening environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by optimizing the AutoCCS algorithm for speed and integrating it with a real-time data processing architecture, we can achieve automated, accurate CCS extraction at a scale compatible with high-throughput screening, thereby enabling CCS to be a routine filter for compound identification in large datasets.
proposals.method: We will develop an optimized AutoCCS pipeline by implementing a just-in-time (JIT) compilation of its core algorithms (e.g., for mobility peak detection and calibration fitting) using the Numba framework in Python to drastically reduce processing time. This optimized engine will be integrated via a custom API with a dual-gated SLIM-Orbitrap MS platform, enabling real-time data streaming. A scheduler will manage batch processing of continuous acquisitions, and results will be fed into a relational database (e.g., PostgreSQL) with CCS values automatically appended to feature tables for immediate statistical analysis in drug or metabolomic screens.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, open-source software pipeline for high-throughput CCS extraction, directly increasing the analytical throughput of advanced IM-MS platforms. It will demonstrate the practical integration of CCS as a real-time filter in large-scale screens, providing a new benchmark for the scale at which high-resolution molecular phenotyping can be performed in biomedical research.
proposals.novelty: While AutoCCS exists, its optimization for real-time, high-throughput operation on streaming data from the most advanced SLIM-Orbitrap platforms is novel. The integration creates a closed-loop system for automated CCS-based screening, moving beyond proof-of-concept to practical utility.

proposals.title: Automated Collision Cross-Section Calculation for Heterogeneous, Multi-Charge State Protein Assemblies Using SLIM Ultra-High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Orbitrap MS
proposals.motivation: Native mass spectrometry, particularly when integrated with ion mobility (IM), is revolutionizing structural biology by probing intact protein complexes. However, automated collision cross-section (CCS) calculation remains limited to well-behaved, single-charge-state systems, failing for heterogeneous assemblies like those in human pathologies (e.g., high-grade serous ovarian cancer) which exhibit broad, overlapping charge state distributions. This gap hinders high-throughput, integrative proteogenomic studies of macromolecular machinery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by coupling high-resolution Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) IM separations with Orbitrap mass analysis and a novel charge-deconvolution algorithm, we can develop a robust, automated pipeline to derive accurate CCS values for large (>1 MDa), non-covalent protein complexes exhibiting multiple coexisting charge states.
proposals.method: We will utilize a dual-gated SLIM-IM-Orbitrap platform to achieve ultra-high-resolution ion mobility separations. An automated data processing pipeline will be developed, building on prior FTICR charge determination logic. This pipeline will implement a novel algorithm that simultaneously deconvolutes charge state distributions and mobility peaks using constrained optimization, correlating arrival time distributions across *m/z* to assign CCS to specific oligomeric states within heterogeneous mixtures, validated against known standards like the 20S proteasome.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first automated software tool for CCS determination of polydisperse protein assemblies, directly enabling large-scale studies of native complex stoichiometry and dynamics. It will provide a critical new data dimension for integrated 'slim' proteogenomic characterizations, linking genetic alterations to changes in macromolecular structure and stability in disease.
proposals.novelty: While SLIM provides exceptional mobility resolution, automated CCS assignment for multi-charge-state complexes is unsolved. Our approach uniquely combines SLIM's gating precision with algorithmic charge/mobility deconvolution derived from historical FTICR charge determination principles, moving beyond single-species analysis to true complex mixture characterization.

proposals.title: A Simulation Framework and Probabilistic Model for Untargeted Detection of Unannotated Peptides in High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Mass Spectrometry Data
proposals.motivation: Current proteomic analyses predominantly rely on matching mass spectrometry (MS) signals to a database of known peptides, a paradigm that systematically overlooks novel sequences, unexpected splice variants, and uncharacterized post-translational modifications (PTMs). This 'known-peptide assumption' is a major bottleneck in discovery proteomics, particularly for understanding disease heterogeneity and cellular adaptation. High-resolution platforms like SLIM-based ultra-high-resolution ion mobility-Orbitrap MS, as pioneered by Smith's group, generate data of sufficient quality to challenge this assumption, but lack the computational frameworks to do so robustly.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling a fraction of MS/MS spectra as originating from peptides not present in reference databases, a probabilistic framework can more accurately characterize proteomic complexity and enable the discovery of novel peptide species directly from high-resolution ion mobility-MS data.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation engine that injects signals from 'unknown' peptides—generated via in silico mutagenesis, non-canonical splicing, and randomized PTMs—into real experimental data from our Dual-gated SLIM-IM-Orbitrap platform. We will then adapt and extend the 'Analysis of Mass Spectrometry' (AMS) probabilistic mixture modeling framework, integrating ion mobility collision cross-section (CCS) as an additional, orthogonal dimension of evidence. The model will treat peptide identity as a latent variable, with a dedicated component for spectra not explained by the canonical database.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated simulation and analysis pipeline designed to quantify and characterize the 'unknown' fraction in proteomic datasets. It will deliver a new open-source tool for the community and a benchmark for evaluating untargeted discovery algorithms. The results will directly inform the sensitivity limits of current discovery workflows and guide future instrument development for comprehensive proteome mapping.
proposals.novelty: While database search tools allow for open modifications, they remain constrained by a parent sequence database. Our approach is novel in its foundational relaxation of the database constraint for a subset of signals and the explicit use of ultra-high-resolution ion mobility data (CCS) within a probabilistic model to deconvolve known from potentially novel peptide ions.

proposals.title: Direct Validation of Flux Balance Analysis Predictions in Ovarian Cancer Metabolism via Integrated Ultra-High-Resolution Proteomics and In Vitro Enzyme Activity Profiling
proposals.motivation: Flux Balance Analysis (FBA) is a cornerstone of systems biology, predicting metabolic flux states from genomic, transcriptomic, and proteomic data. However, a critical gap exists between predicted enzyme presence from proteomics and actual functional activity, as post-translational modifications, allosteric regulation, and metabolite concentrations can decouple protein abundance from catalytic rate. This disconnect limits the predictive power of metabolic models in complex diseases like cancer, where understanding functional metabolic rewiring is essential.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating direct, quantitative measurements of key enzyme activities with ultra-deep, high-resolution proteomic data will reveal significant discrepancies with FBA predictions in high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSOC) models, and that these discrepancies will identify critical regulatory nodes not captured by protein abundance alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage our established platform combining Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) for high-resolution ion mobility separations with ultra-high-field Orbitrap mass spectrometry for deep, quantitative proteomics of HGSOC cell lines and patient-derived organoids. Predicted high-flux enzymes from FBA models (built from this proteomic and prior transcriptomic data) will be targeted for functional validation. Their specific activities will be measured using optimized in vitro coupled spectrophotometric or fluorometric assays on clarified lysates, quantifying reaction rates (Vmax) under defined substrate saturation. Activity-to-abundance ratios will be calculated and compared across metabolic conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, systematic validation of FBA flux predictions using matched proteomic and enzyme activity data in a cancer model. It will establish a novel benchmark dataset correlating protein abundance with catalytic function and identify specific metabolic enzymes where regulation occurs primarily at the activity level. This will lead to more accurate, mechanism-aware constraint-based metabolic models.
proposals.novelty: While proteogenomic characterization of HGSOC exists, the direct integration of ultra-high-resolution proteomic profiles with functional enzyme activity assays to test core systems biology predictions is novel. The use of the SLIM-Orbitrap platform provides the proteomic depth and specificity required to quantify low-abundance metabolic enzymes, enabling this precise functional comparison.

proposals.title: Functional Interrogation of Host-Derived Proteins in Virion Preparations via Integrated SLIM-IM-Orbitrap Mass Spectrometry and Polarized Ion Mobility Analysis
proposals.motivation: In virology and proteomics, mass spectrometry analyses of purified virions consistently identify numerous host cell proteins. These are routinely dismissed as passive contaminants from the purification process, potentially obscuring functionally significant incorporations that influence viral replication, immune evasion, or pathogenesis. A rigorous, high-resolution analytical framework is needed to systematically distinguish mere co-purification from bona fide virion association and function.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant subset of host-derived proteins identified in purified virion preparations are not passive contaminants but are selectively incorporated, exhibiting specific stoichiometries, structural orientations, or post-translational modifications indicative of functional roles in the viral lifecycle.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual-gated Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) module coupled to an ultra-high-resolution Orbitrap mass spectrometer for deep, reproducible virion proteomics. High-field asymmetric waveform ion mobility spectrometry (FAIMS) will be integrated for orthogonal separation. The key innovation is applying polarized ion mobility analysis—measuring the orientationally averaged collision cross-sections of ions under different polarization conditions—to probe for consistent structural motifs or complexes between viral and host proteins, moving beyond mere abundance. Data will be analyzed using algorithms for co-elution profile correlation and stoichiometric modeling against rigorous negative controls (e.g., mock-infected cell supernatants).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive analytical pipeline to critically evaluate host protein presence in virions, shifting the field from a contaminant-centric to a function-centric view. It will yield a new resource of high-confidence candidate host factors for virological validation and demonstrate the power of integrated ultra-high-resolution ion mobility-mass spectrometry for complex biological assemblies.
proposals.novelty: While SLIM-IM-MS platforms exist, their application combined with polarized mobility analysis to dissect host-virus protein complexes is novel. This approach moves beyond cataloging proteins to probing their structural context within the virion, a significant advance over standard proteomic contaminant filtering.

proposals.title: High-Resolution Temporal and Host-Cell Resolved Proteomic Profiling of Virion Assembly Reveals Dynamic Composition
proposals.motivation: Viral proteomics has traditionally assumed a static virion proteome, potentially obscuring critical mechanisms of pathogenesis, immune evasion, and host adaptation. This assumption limits our understanding of how virion composition, including host-derived proteins, varies during infection progression or across different host cell types, which is crucial for antiviral strategies and vaccine design.
proposals.hypothesis: The proteomic composition of mature virions is not uniform but varies dynamically as a function of infection time and the specific host cell type from which the virion is produced.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual-gated SLIM (Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations) ultra-high-field ion mobility device coupled to an Orbitrap mass spectrometer for deep, high-resolution proteomic analysis. Virions will be purified from distinct host cell lines (e.g., epithelial vs. immune) at precise post-infection timepoints. Data-independent acquisition (DIA) mass spectrometry will be used for comprehensive peptide quantification, with differential abundance analysis performed using the `limma`-voom pipeline adapted for proteomics to statistically identify host- and time-dependent virion proteome changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm of 'dynamic virion proteomics,' directly linking host cell physiology and infection kinetics to virion composition. It will provide a high-fidelity map of host proteins selectively incorporated into virions, identifying novel determinants of viral tropism and potential host-derived vulnerabilities for therapeutic targeting.
proposals.novelty: While prior proteogenomic studies have catalogued virion components, this proposal uniquely applies ultra-high-resolution ion mobility-MS and rigorous temporal/cellular resolution to test the core assumption of uniformity, moving from a static parts list to a dynamic model of virion assembly.

proposals.title: Functional Interrogation of Low-Abundance Virion Proteins via High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Mass Spectrometry and CRISPR-Cas9 Perturbation
proposals.motivation: Current proteogenomic analyses of viruses often assume that protein abundance within the virion is a direct proxy for functional importance, potentially overlooking critical roles of low-abundance proteins in infection and pathogenesis. This assumption is untested and may lead to significant gaps in understanding viral mechanisms and identifying therapeutic targets, particularly for complex viruses like herpesviruses or coronaviruses where virion composition is intricate.
proposals.hypothesis: Low-abundance proteins incorporated into the mature virion are essential for specific, high-impact stages of the viral life cycle, such as immune evasion, host cell entry, or genome delivery, and their functional importance is disproportionate to their molar quantity.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual-gated SLIM (Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations)-Ion Mobility-Orbitrap MS platform to achieve ultra-high-resolution separation and identification of the complete virion proteome from purified human cytomegalovirus (HCMV). This will be integrated with a CRISPR-Cas9 functional screen, where host cells are edited to individually knock out genes encoding each identified low-abundance virion protein, followed by high-content imaging to quantify defects in viral entry, replication, and spread. Data integration will use a custom algorithm correlating precise protein abundance (from FTICR-like mass measurement accuracy) with phenotypic impact scores from the screen.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, quantitative framework for assessing virion protein function independent of abundance, challenging a prevailing heuristic in virology. It will identify novel, essential low-abundance viral factors as potential targets for antiviral intervention and demonstrate the power of integrating ultra-high-resolution MS with functional genomics for complex biological assemblies.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard abundance-function paradigm in virology by systematically testing the necessity of low-abundance virion components, a hypothesis rarely pursued. The technical approach is novel in combining the unparalleled separation power of SLIM-IM-MS for comprehensive virion proteomics with a targeted, gene-specific functional screen, moving beyond correlative abundance measurements to causative functional analysis.

proposals.title: Absolute Quantification and Functional Significance of gB and gM Glycoprotein Abundance in Herpesviruses Using SLIM-Enabled Ultra-High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: In herpesviruses, glycoproteins like gB and gM are critical for viral entry and assembly, but their precise stoichiometry in the virion envelope remains poorly quantified. Pre-2026, relative abundance measurements from proteomic studies often suggest gM is more abundant than gB, a finding with major implications for understanding virion structure and morphogenesis. Validating this hierarchy and exploring its biological basis is essential for advancing fundamental virology and identifying potential therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: The glycoprotein gM is definitively more abundant than gB in mature herpesvirus virions, and this stoichiometric ratio is conserved across alphaherpesviruses (e.g., HSV-1) and is functionally linked to efficient viral egress and secondary envelopment.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual-gated Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) module coupled to an ultra-high-resolution Orbitrap or FTICR mass spectrometer for high-throughput, high-mobility analysis of intact glycoprotein complexes and peptides. Viral particles will be purified via gradient centrifugation, and proteins digested with specific proteases. Using isotopically labeled internal standards for absolute quantification (AQUA), we will perform targeted proteogenomic analysis. Data-independent acquisition (DIA) and ion mobility filtering will deconvolute complex spectra, while custom algorithms will align mobility profiles with isotopic distributions for precise abundance ratios.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first absolute, validated stoichiometry of key herpesvirus envelope glycoproteins, moving beyond relative spectral counts. It will establish a high-precision SLIM-IM-MS workflow for challenging viral membrane proteomes. Biologically, it will link quantified protein abundance to functional outcomes via correlative microscopy and mutant virus studies, clarifying virion assembly mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: The integration of SLIM-based ultra-high-resolution ion mobility for intact glycoprotein separation with absolute quantification mass spectrometry to solve a long-standing virological stoichiometry question is novel. This approach moves beyond bulk proteomics to analyze native-like complexes from purified virions with unprecedented resolution and accuracy.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Virion Proteomics: Resolving Host-Cell-Dependent Heterogeneity in Viral Particle Composition via SLIM-Guided Ultra-High-Resolution Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: Viral populations are heterogeneous, and virion composition is influenced by the host cell's proteomic state, impacting infectivity, immune evasion, and pathogenesis. Bulk analyses average out this critical single-cell-level variation. Pre-2026, while single-cell proteomics is emerging, applying it to the analysis of intact virions produced from individual infected cells remains a formidable, unsolved challenge due to extreme sample limitations and analytical sensitivity requirements.
proposals.hypothesis: Virions produced from single, genetically identical host cells under uniform infection conditions will exhibit significant, measurable proteome heterogeneity, reflecting stochastic host-cell processes and creating distinct viral particle subpopulations.
proposals.method: We will scale down virion production using microfluidic nanowells to isolate and culture single cells, followed by on-chip infection and virion collection. Virion proteins will be digested, and peptides analyzed using our established platform integrating Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) for ultra-high-resolution ion mobility pre-separation and filtering, coupled to a high-field Orbitrap mass spectrometer. We will employ 'polarized' ion gating strategies within the SLIM device to selectively enrich ions from the target virion digest. Data-independent acquisition (DIA) will be used for deep proteome profiling, and data will be processed with algorithms for matching ultra-high-resolution mobility profiles (CCS values) to peptide identifications, enabling confident measurement from ultra-low inputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first analytical framework for single-cell virion proteomics, providing a direct measurement of host-cell-dependent virion variation. It will generate a foundational dataset linking host cell state to virion composition, offering new insights into viral life cycle bottlenecks and the origins of particle heterogeneity. The developed platform will push the sensitivity limits of integrated ion mobility-mass spectrometry.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious integration of single-cell virology with SLIM-based high-resolution ion mobility and FT-based mass analysis to deconvolute proteomic heterogeneity at the level of individual viral particles, moving beyond bulk or population-averaged virion measurements.

proposals.title: A Comprehensive, Quantitative Proteomic Atlas of Human Herpesvirus Lytic Infections Using High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Orbitrap Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: Human herpesviruses (HHVs) establish lifelong infections and are associated with cancers, neurological disorders, and complications in immunocompromised individuals. While proteomic studies exist for individual viruses like HSV-1 or HCMV, a systematic, comparative analysis across all eight HHVs using a unified, state-of-the-art platform is lacking. Such a dataset is critical for identifying conserved viral manipulation strategies, unique virus-specific host pathways, and potential pan-herpesviral therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a standardized, high-resolution proteogenomic pipeline to cells lytically infected with all eight human herpesviruses will reveal a core set of dysregulated host pathways common to herpesviral infection, as well as distinct, virus-specific proteomic signatures that correlate with their unique clinical pathologies.
proposals.method: We will infect permissive cell lines with each of the eight HHVs, performing biological triplicates. Proteins will be digested with trypsin and analyzed using our established gel-free LC-MS/MS pipeline centered on the dual-gated SLIM (Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations)-ion mobility-Orbitrap platform. This provides ultra-high-resolution separations and mass measurements. Data will be processed using the FragPipe computational platform with MSFragger for sensitive database searching against combined human and virus-specific proteogenomic databases, followed by quantification via IonQuant. Statistical analysis will use the Perseus suite for ANOVA and hierarchical clustering.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first directly comparable, high-resolution proteomic atlas for all human herpesviruses, serving as a foundational resource for the virology community. It will identify both pan-herpesviral and virus-specific host protein interaction networks, offering new hypotheses for mechanisms of pathogenesis and latency. The data will also refine viral open reading frame annotations, enhancing reference databases for future diagnostic and vaccine efforts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in the study of a single virus, but in the systematic, comparative application of a unified, ultra-high-resolution SLIM-ion mobility-Orbitrap MS platform across the entire herpesvirus family, enabling direct quantitative comparisons previously confounded by methodological variability between studies.

proposals.title: Spatially Resolved Proteogenomic Profiling of Tumor Microenvironments via Integrated SLIM-IMS-MS and High-Definition Spatial Transcriptomics
proposals.motivation: Current proteogenomic studies of cancer, such as those for high-grade serous ovarian cancer, often rely on bulk tissue analysis, which obscures critical spatial heterogeneity within the tumor architecture. The tumor microenvironment contains distinct cellular neighborhoods where discordant RNA and protein expression drives progression and therapy resistance. Pre-2026, spatial transcriptomics (e.g., 10x Genomics Visium) maps RNA, but a correlative, high-resolution spatial map of the proteome is lacking, creating a major gap in functional understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating ultra-high-resolution ion mobility-mass spectrometry (IMS-MS) proteomic data with spatial transcriptomic maps will reveal spatially defined, post-transcriptionally regulated protein modules within the tumor microenvironment that are invisible to bulk or single-modality spatial analyses.
proposals.method: We will use 10x Visium spatial transcriptomics on serial sections of human ovarian tumor tissue. Adjacent sections will be subjected to high-resolution proteomic analysis using laser capture microdissection to isolate regions of interest, followed by nanoLC-MS/MS on a platform integrating Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) for ultra-high-resolution ion mobility separation with an Orbitrap mass analyzer. Data integration will employ a custom computational pipeline using non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) to deconvolute region-specific multi-omic signatures and a graph-based alignment algorithm to co-register proteomic and transcriptomic spatial features.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first spatially resolved proteogenomic atlas of high-grade serous ovarian cancer, directly linking RNA expression landscapes to functional protein modules in histological context. It will establish a novel analytical framework for identifying spatially driven biological mechanisms, such as localized immune evasion or metabolic symbiosis, offering new targets for spatially informed therapeutic strategies.
proposals.novelty: While spatial transcriptomics and high-resolution IMS-MS exist separately, their direct integration via a SLIM-IMS-MS platform and tailored computational co-registration to achieve spatially resolved proteogenomics at near-cellular resolution is a novel and untested approach that moves beyond correlative bulk tissue studies.

proposals.title: Deep Learning-Driven Multi-Omic Integration of SLIM-IM-Orbitrap Proteomics with Genomic and Transcriptomic Data for High-Resolution Subtype Discovery in Ovarian Cancer
proposals.motivation: High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSOC) exhibits significant molecular heterogeneity, complicating prognosis and treatment. While proteogenomic studies have revealed insights, the integration of ultra-high-resolution proteomic data from advanced platforms like SLIM-IM-Orbitrap MS with genomic and transcriptomic data remains a major computational challenge. Pre-2026, methods for fusing these disparate, high-dimensional data types are limited, hindering the discovery of robust, biologically coherent subtypes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a deep neural network architecture can effectively integrate genomic, transcriptomic, and ultra-high-resolution proteomic data to reveal novel, clinically relevant molecular subtypes of HGSOC that are not discernible from single-omic analyses.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-modal deep learning framework using a combination of autoencoders and attention mechanisms. Genomic (mutations, CNVs), transcriptomic (RNA-seq), and proteomic data (from our SLIM-IM-Orbitrap platform) will be processed through separate encoder networks to learn latent representations. These will be fused using a cross-attention layer to model interactions, followed by a clustering layer (e.g., Deep Embedded Clustering) for subtype discovery. The model will be trained and validated on existing HGSOC cohorts with matched multi-omics data.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational pipeline for integrating cutting-edge, high-resolution proteomic data with other omics layers. It will produce a refined molecular taxonomy of HGSOC with potential prognostic and therapeutic implications, directly extending our prior proteogenomic characterization. The deep learning framework will be a generalizable tool for the mass spectrometry community.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in its specific design to handle the unique data structure and high dimensionality of ion mobility-resolved proteomic data from SLIM-IM-Orbitrap platforms within a multi-omic integration model, moving beyond standard bulk omics fusion to leverage the latest pre-2026 separations technology.

proposals.title: A Pan-Cancer Proteogenomic Atlas: Systematic Integration of Ultra-High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Mass Spectrometry and Genomics to Define Shared and Lineage-Specific Oncogenic Drivers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, large-scale cancer genomics projects like TCGA have cataloged genomic alterations, but a comprehensive understanding of functional protein-level consequences across cancer types is lacking. Integrated proteogenomic studies, such as the one on ovarian cancer by Smith et al., have proven powerful but are limited to single cancer types, preventing the identification of pan-cancer protein-based pathways and tissue-specific drivers. A systematic, multi-cancer analysis is needed to translate genomic discoveries into a unified functional view of oncogenesis.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a standardized, high-resolution proteogenomic pipeline across multiple cancer types will reveal a core set of common dysregulated protein pathways underlying fundamental oncogenic processes, alongside distinct, lineage-specific proteomic drivers that determine clinical phenotype.
proposals.method: We will scale the established pipeline from our ovarian cancer study to 10+ additional TCGA cancer types with available genomic data. Proteomic analysis will utilize our dual-gated SLIM-based ultra-high-resolution ion mobility-Orbitrap MS platform for deep, reproducible profiling. Data integration will employ the CPTAC proteogenomic workflow, aligning MS data with genomic alterations (mutations, CNV) using tools like MSFragger and Philosopher for identification/quantification, and custom algorithms for phosphoproteomic pathway activation mapping. Statistical comparison across cancers will use hierarchical clustering and multivariate analysis to segregate common from unique signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale, standardized pan-cancer proteogenomic atlas, directly linking genomic variation to functional protein expression and modification. It will identify novel, pharmacologically targetable protein complexes common across cancers and define lineage-specific proteomic biomarkers for precise diagnosis. The resource will serve as a foundational dataset for the cancer biology community.
proposals.novelty: While single-cancer proteogenomics exists, the systematic application of a single, ultra-high-resolution MS platform (SLIM-IM-Orbitrap) across a pan-cancer cohort to ensure directly comparable, high-fidelity protein measurements is unprecedented. This approach moves beyond genomic correlation to functionally define conserved and divergent oncogenic modules at the protein level.

proposals.title: Inferring Somatic Genomic Alterations from Deep Proteomic Profiles Using High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Mass Spectrometry and Multi-Modal Neural Networks
proposals.motivation: In cancer biology, genomic alterations are primary drivers, but their functional consequences are mediated by proteins. Current diagnostics often rely on genomic sequencing, which can miss post-transcriptional regulatory effects and is not always feasible. A method to deduce the underlying genomic cause directly from the proteome would provide a functional, integrated view of disease state, crucial for understanding and targeting cancers like high-grade serous ovarian cancer where proteogenomic discordance is common.
proposals.hypothesis: High-fidelity, deep-coverage proteomic profiles, measured using ultra-high-resolution ion mobility-mass spectrometry, contain sufficient information to accurately predict the presence of specific somatic genomic alterations, including point mutations, copy number variations, and gene fusions.
proposals.method: We will leverage Dr. Smith's expertise in Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) and high-field asymmetric waveform ion mobility spectrometry (FAIMS) coupled to Orbitrap/FTICR mass spectrometry to generate ultra-high-resolution proteomic profiles from well-characterized cancer cell lines and patient-derived xenografts with known genomic landscapes. A multi-modal neural network architecture will be trained on these paired proteogenomic datasets. The model will integrate features like protein expression levels, post-translational modification patterns, and proteoform profiles (from large individual ion measurements) to predict discrete genomic alteration events, using attention mechanisms to identify the most informative proteomic signatures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel computational framework for the 'inverse problem' in proteogenomics, directly linking proteomic observables to genomic causes. It will demonstrate the potential of deep proteomics as a standalone functional diagnostic for genomic instability. The resulting models and validated proteomic signatures will provide new biological insights into how specific mutations manifest in the proteome.
proposals.novelty: While proteogenomic correlation studies exist, the explicit inverse modeling—predicting genomics from proteomics alone—is largely unexplored. The approach is uniquely enabled by the unprecedented depth and quality of proteomic data from Dr. Smith's advanced ion mobility-mass spectrometry platforms, which capture proteoform-level information critical for distinguishing subtle genomic effects.

proposals.title: A High-Field Asymmetric Waveform Ion Mobility Spectrometry (FAIMS) and Desalting Ion Funnel Interface for Robust Native ESI-MS of Proteins from Complex Buffers
proposals.motivation: Native electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) is transformative for analyzing protein complexes, but its application is severely limited by the requirement for volatile, low-salt buffers. This necessitates extensive sample purification, which is time-consuming, can disrupt non-covalent interactions, and hinders the direct analysis of proteins from physiological or chromatographic eluents. Developing methods to tolerate high salt and contaminants would enable direct analysis of proteins and complexes from crude cell lysates or standard biochemical buffers, dramatically accelerating structural proteomics and systems biology workflows.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a novel, multi-stage ion funnel interface incorporating high-field asymmetric waveform ion mobility spectrometry (FAIMS) for gas-phase separation with selective ion filtering will effectively remove adducted salt and contaminant ions, thereby preserving the signal of intact protein complexes from samples with non-volatile buffers.
proposals.method: We will design and construct a dual ion funnel interface between the ESI source and the mass analyzer. The first funnel will operate at elevated pressures (10-30 Torr) to act as a desolvation and declustering region, using optimized RF and DC potentials to collimate ions while promoting the dissociation of loosely bound salt adducts. Ions will then be passed into a FAIMS device, where the asymmetric waveform and compensation voltage (CV) will be systematically optimized to selectively transmit protein ions while filtering out smaller, highly mobile contaminant ions and salt clusters. This filtered ion beam will be focused by a second ion funnel into the high-resolution mass spectrometer (an Orbitrap or FTICR platform). We will develop automated CV stepping routines synchronized with MS acquisition to profile and optimize transmission for different protein charge states.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust instrumental method for performing native MS on samples containing high levels of non-volatile salts and contaminants, reducing or eliminating the need for buffer exchange. It will provide a new toolkit for direct analysis of protein complexes from cellular fractions and standard biochemical preparations, advancing the field of native mass spectrometry towards more high-throughput and biologically relevant applications.
proposals.novelty: While FAIMS is used for small molecule separations and desalting funnels exist, their combined, targeted application for preserving high-mass, low-charge-state protein complexes from complex buffers is novel. The innovation lies in the specific tuning of the FAIMS CV and funnel pressures to achieve a 'gas-phase purification' that maintains non-covalent interactions, moving beyond simple declustering.

proposals.title: Systematic Mapping of Electrospray Ionization Landscapes: A Combined Ion Mobility-Orbitrap MS Study of Solvent and Voltage Effects Across Diverse Protein Classes
proposals.motivation: Electrospray ionization (ESI) is foundational for native and top-down mass spectrometry, yet ionization efficiency varies drastically across proteins, compromising sensitivity and quantitative accuracy. Current methods often rely on ad-hoc optimization, lacking a systematic framework to predict optimal ESI conditions for different protein classes (e.g., membrane, intrinsically disordered, large complexes). This empirical gap limits the robustness of high-throughput proteogenomic and structural analyses, such as those in cancer biomarker discovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for distinct protein classes, defined by molecular weight, hydrophobicity, and structural stability, there exist predictable and non-overlapping optimal combinations of solvent composition (e.g., aqueous/organic ratio, additive type) and ESI voltage that maximize ionization efficiency and preserve native-like structures.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual-gated Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) module coupled to an ultra-high-resolution Orbitrap mass spectrometer, as recently developed in our lab. A diverse protein library (n>50) will be analyzed across a multi-dimensional grid of solvent compositions (varying methanol/water ratios, acetic acid, ammonium acetate) and ESI voltages. Ionization efficiency will be quantified via absolute ion current measurements, while charge state distributions and collisional cross-sections from SLIM-IMS will assess structural preservation. Data will be analyzed using multivariate regression and clustering algorithms to identify condition-protein class correlations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first comprehensive, publicly accessible database mapping ESI response surfaces for diverse proteins, directly informing method development for targeted assays. It will establish rational guidelines for selecting MS conditions based on protein properties, enhancing sensitivity and reproducibility in biomarker verification and structural proteomics.
proposals.novelty: While solvent and voltage effects are known, a systematic, high-resolution IMS-MS study linking these parameters to quantitative ionization efficiency and structural metrics across a broad, defined protein library is novel. The use of the SLIM-Orbitrap platform for this precise application, combining ultra-high-resolution mass analysis with high-fidelity mobility separation for condition optimization, represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: A High-Density Nano-ESI Emitter Array for Parallelized, High-Throughput Clinical Proteomic Screening
proposals.motivation: The translation of mass spectrometry-based proteomics into routine clinical screening is bottlenecked by low sample throughput. Electrospray ionization (ESI)-MS remains a largely serial technique, ill-suited for analyzing the hundreds to thousands of samples required for robust biomarker discovery or population-scale studies. A transformative leap in analytical throughput is needed to match the scale of genomic microarray platforms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a microfabricated chip containing a high-density array of individually addressable nano-ESI emitters, integrated with automated positional control, can transform ESI-MS into a parallelized platform, enabling order-of-magnitude increases in sample analysis rates for clinical proteomics.
proposals.method: We will design and fabricate a silicon-glass 'MS Array Chip' featuring a 10x10 grid of monolithic, electrically isolated nano-ESI emitters. Each emitter will be coupled to a dedicated microfluidic channel for sample introduction from a source plate via an integrated liquid handler. The chip will be mounted on a high-speed, high-precision XY stage interfaced with a high-resolution ion mobility-Orbitrap MS (e.g., SLIM-enabled platforms). Custom software will synchronize stage movement, emitter voltage gating, and MS acquisition to perform rapid (e.g., <5 seconds per position), automated sequencing across the array with minimal intersample carryover.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a first-generation hardware and software platform for massively parallelized ESI-MS analysis, directly addressing the throughput crisis in clinical proteomics. It will establish a new paradigm for sample introduction, moving from serial infusion or LC-MS/MS to spatially multiplexed analysis, enabling screening campaigns of unprecedented scale.
proposals.novelty: While multiplexed ESI sources exist, this approach uniquely adapts the core parallelization and spatial addressing architecture of DNA microarrays to MS, creating a high-density, individually addressable emitter array for true sample multiplexing rather than mere source multiplexing. The integration of high-resolution ion mobility (SLIM) and FTMS with this platform for parallelized, high-fidelity analysis is unprecedented.

proposals.title: Computational Design of Ionization-Antenna Proteins for Enhanced Electrospray Ionization Efficiency in Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: Electrospray ionization (ESI) efficiency varies dramatically between different proteins, limiting detection sensitivity and quantitative accuracy in proteomics and structural biology. This variability is poorly understood and not systematically controlled. A rational design framework to optimize proteins for ESI would transform bottom-up and native MS analyses, particularly for low-abundance targets.
proposals.hypothesis: Protein sequences and surface structures can be engineered, analogous to RF antenna design, to optimally couple with the electrostatic and hydrodynamic environment of the ESI process, thereby maximizing the yield of stable, gas-phase ions.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline integrating molecular dynamics (MD) simulations of proteins in charged nanodroplets (using GROMACS with enhanced electrostatic fields) with finite-element modeling of the ESI plume (COMSOL). Machine learning models (graph neural networks) will be trained on MD-derived surface charge distributions and experimental ESI-MS response data to predict ionization efficiency. This will enable the design of de novo protein 'antenna' domains or tags with optimized charge localization and hydrophobicity patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first principled framework for designing proteins and tags for optimal ESI response. It will deliver novel, high-efficiency ionization tags for proteomic applications and provide fundamental insights into the molecular determinants of successful ion formation, advancing both analytical technology and basic understanding of the ESI process.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct analogy to RF antenna engineering, treating ESI as a transduction problem, and in its integrative computational strategy combining MD, fluid dynamics, and machine learning specifically for ionization optimization—a departure from traditional, empirical MS sample preparation.

proposals.title: Spatially Targeted Protein Liberation and Ionization via Engineered Cleavable Tags and Directed Chemical Probes for High-Sensitivity Tissue Imaging Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: Current mass spectrometry imaging (MSI) techniques for tissue proteomics, such as MALDI, struggle with sensitivity and specificity for low-abundance proteins and post-translational modifications (PTMs) without extensive sample preparation that destroys spatial context. There is a critical need for methods that can selectively enhance the ionization and detection of specific protein targets directly from complex tissue matrices to map signaling complexes and PTM states within native architecture.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that proteins engineered with a fusion tag containing a labile chemical linker and an ionization-enhancing motif can be selectively liberated and ionized with high efficiency directly from tissue upon localized delivery of a complementary cleavage reagent, enabling spatially resolved, sensitive detection of targeted proteins.
proposals.method: We will engineer a panel of protein tags incorporating (1) a high-proton-affinity 'slim' peptide motif to boost ESI efficiency, (2) a cleavable linker (e.g., photocleavable or enzyme-specific), and (3) a protein-binding domain (e.g., nanobody). These will be expressed in cell models. For tissue analysis, we will integrate a micro-capillary ESI emitter with a coaxial sheath flow for delivering UV light or enzymes as the cleavage trigger, building on SLIM and dual-gated ion mobility platforms. Upon spatially directed probe contact, the cleavage event will release tagged proteins for immediate ionization, followed by ultra-high-resolution analysis using our existing FTICR and Orbitrap platforms coupled with high-field ion mobility for separation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for targeted in situ proteomics by co-optimizing molecular design and instrumental activation. It will deliver a method for multiplexed, on-demand mapping of low-abundance protein forms directly from tissue, significantly enhancing sensitivity and spatial specificity over broad-ablation techniques. The developed tags and probe system will be a versatile toolkit for probing protein complexes and PTM dynamics in cancer and other tissues.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of protein engineering for enhanced ionization with a spatially directed chemical cleavage probe for on-tissue release is a novel, synergistic approach distinct from passive ablation or label-free MSI.
