It moves beyond existing tag methods by integrating the liberation and ionization steps into a single, spatially controlled event at the probe tip.

proposals.title: Rational Design of Protein Sequences and Tags for Enhanced Electrospray Ionization and Ultra-High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Mass Spectrometry Analysis
proposals.motivation: Electrospray ionization-mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) is central to proteomics, but ionization efficiency and spectral complexity vary dramatically between proteins, limiting sensitivity and quantitative accuracy. Current approaches are largely observational, lacking a predictive framework to engineer proteins for optimal MS analysis. This gap hinders the development of targeted assays for low-abundance proteins and the design of synthetic protein standards.
proposals.hypothesis: Protein sequences and surface properties can be rationally engineered, or modular tags can be designed, to systematically enhance ESI ionization efficiency, produce predictable charge state distributions, and improve ion mobility separation, thereby optimizing detection and measurement by ultra-high-resolution MS platforms.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing high-resolution datasets from SLIM-IMS-Orbitrap and FTICR platforms to train a machine learning model (e.g., a gradient-boosted tree or shallow neural network) correlating protein sequence/structural features (e.g., pI, surface polarity, charge distribution) with observed ionization metrics. This model will guide the in silico design of protein variants and novel, genetically encodable 'ESI-optimizer tags' rich in basic residues. Designed constructs will be expressed, and their MS performance (signal intensity, charge state envelope, IMS arrival time distribution) will be rigorously evaluated using our established dual-gated SLIM-IMS-Orbitrap platform, comparing them to wild-type counterparts.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first principled, data-driven framework for the forward engineering of proteins for mass spectrometry. It will deliver a set of validated design rules and novel protein tags that significantly boost MS sensitivity. This will directly enable more sensitive detection of low-abundance cellular proteins and improve the fidelity of absolute quantification using engineered protein standards.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized native ionization behavior, this proposal inverts the problem to actively design proteins for the instrument. It uniquely combines high-resolution IMS-MS datasets, predictive computational modeling, and protein engineering in a closed feedback loop, moving beyond descriptive analysis to prescriptive design.

proposals.title: Spatially Programmed Bioorthogonal Ligation for Direct Tissue Proteomics via High-Resolution Ion Mobility-ESI-MS
proposals.motivation: Direct mass spectrometric analysis of tissue sections, such as by DESI or MALDI imaging, struggles with the immense complexity of the proteome, limiting identification and multiplexing. Pre-2026, spatial proteomics largely relies on antibody-based methods, which are low-plex and require prior knowledge. A method to chemically encode spatial and protein identity information directly on-tissue for MS readout is critically needed to bridge spatial biology and deep proteomic profiling.
proposals.hypothesis: That a library of ESI-MS-compatible, bioorthogonal chemical probes, applied to tissue in a pre-defined spatial array, will form covalent conjugates with specific protein functional groups, enabling their multiplexed identification and spatial mapping via high-resolution mass spectrometry of the cleaved probe tags.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of sulfonate ester or fluorophenyl ester-based probes bearing distinct mass tags and a terminal azide or strained alkyne for subsequent cleavage via CuAAC or SPANC. Probes will be arrayed onto fresh-frozen tissue sections using a microfluidic stamp. After incubation, unreacted probes are washed away, and the tissue is subjected to on-tissue cleavage/release of the probe tags. The released tags, reporting on their protein conjugate's original location, will be analyzed by nano-desorption ESI coupled to the team's established high-field asymmetric waveform ion mobility spectrometry (FAIMS) and ultra-high-resolution Orbitrap platform, using precise mass and mobility for tag identification and spatial reconstruction.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel chemically directed framework for spatially resolved, multiplexed protein analysis directly from tissue, circumventing antibody limitations. It will contribute a new class of MS-compatible bioorthogonal probes and a scalable stamping-MS workflow, advancing the field of spatial proteomics towards higher multiplexity and molecular specificity.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious integration of programmable spatial probe application (chemical scaling) with bioorthogonal tagging and ultra-high-resolution MS for direct tissue analysis, moving beyond passive molecular imaging to active, target-class-specific chemical capture and encoding.

proposals.title: A High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Orbitrap Platform with Nano-ESI Probes for Spatially Resolved, In-Situ Top-Down Proteomics of Tissue Sections
proposals.motivation: Direct, spatially resolved analysis of intact proteins from tissue surfaces remains a major challenge in mass spectrometry-based molecular histology. Pre-2026 methods like MALDI imaging are limited in proteoform coverage and identification confidence, while liquid extraction techniques compromise spatial resolution. A method for in-situ, high-confidence identification and localization of proteoforms would transform our understanding of cellular heterogeneity in disease contexts like cancer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a nano-electrospray ionization (nano-ESI) micro-sampling probe with ultra-high-resolution ion mobility-Orbitrap mass spectrometry will enable the direct, spatially targeted analysis of intact proteins from tissue, providing unambiguous proteoform identification and localization without extensive sample preparation.
proposals.method: We will develop a closed-loop, positionable nano-ESI probe with a solvent-delivery and aspiration system for localized micro-extraction of proteins from fresh-frozen or formalin-fixed tissue sections. The extracted analytes will be ionized directly at the probe tip. The ions will be analyzed using a dual-gated Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) module coupled to an Orbitrap mass spectrometer, as pioneered in our group, to provide ultra-high-resolution ion mobility separations prior to MS analysis. Data acquisition will utilize individual ion mass spectrometry (I2MS) principles for charge state determination of large molecules, and spectral interpretation will rely on top-down fragmentation (e.g., ECD/ETD) and the 'Slim' software suite for proteoform matching.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel platform for spatially resolved top-down proteomics, delivering maps of specific proteoforms (e.g., isoforms, cleavage products, PTM patterns) directly from tissue. It will provide a critical bridge between high-resolution molecular analysis and histological context, enabling new studies of tumor microenvironments and cellular signaling directly from native tissue architecture.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct coupling of a micro-sampling ESI probe for spatial targeting with the unparalleled resolving power of SLIM-Orbitrap-I2MS for intact protein analysis, moving beyond lipid/metabolite imaging to achieve in-situ, high-confidence proteoformomics.

proposals.title: Single-Run Deep Proteomics: Achieving Comprehensive 10-Cell Coverage via SLIM Ultra-High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Orbitrap MS and Polarized Ion Detection
proposals.motivation: Match Between Runs (MBR) is a critical but error-prone step in label-free quantitative proteomics, especially for ultra-low-input samples like single-cell or 10-cell analyses. MBR introduces missing value artifacts and cross-sample contamination of identifications, compromising quantitative accuracy and limiting the ability to study true biological variation in small cell populations. Eliminating this reliance is essential for robust, high-fidelity proteomics of limited clinical and biological specimens.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating ultra-high-resolution ion mobility separations via Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) with polarized ion detection for enhanced signal-to-noise and advanced data-independent acquisition (DIA) schemes, we can achieve sufficient peak capacity and sensitivity to detect and quantify the deep proteome from 10 human cells within a single LC-MS/MS run, obviating the need for cross-run feature matching.
proposals.method: We will construct a dual-gated SLIM-Orbitrap platform, building on Smith's prior work, to provide ultra-high-resolution ion mobility separations. We will implement a polarized ion detection system, using controlled electric fields to enhance the transmission and detection efficiency of low-abundance ions. Data acquisition will use a novel, extended-window DIA scheme (e.g., 2-4 m/z windows) optimized for the platform's peak capacity. Data analysis will leverage in-house algorithms for deconvolution and quantification directly from the high-resolution mobility-mass-charge space, without alignment to external runs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated method and platform for deep, single-run proteome profiling of 10-cell samples, providing a definitive solution to the MBR problem. It will establish a new benchmark for quantitative accuracy and completeness in ultra-low-input proteomics, directly enabling studies of cellular heterogeneity in cancer and development where sample is severely limited.
proposals.novelty: While high-resolution platforms exist, the specific integration of polarized ion optics with SLIM super-resolution for the explicit purpose of eliminating MBR in 10-cell proteomics is unprecedented. The approach moves beyond incremental improvements in alignment algorithms to a hardware-centric solution that redefines the completeness possible within a single analytical run.

proposals.title: DNA-Encoded Antibody Library Pre-enrichment Coupled to High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Orbitrap MS for Deep, Targeted Plasma Proteomics
proposals.motivation: Mass spectrometry (MS)-based plasma proteomics is critically limited by the immense dynamic range of protein concentrations, which obscures low-abundance biomarkers. Pre-2026 affinity-based depletion or enrichment methods lack the multiplexing capacity and specificity needed to target dozens to hundreds of low-abundance proteins simultaneously without cross-reactivity. This bottleneck hinders the discovery and validation of protein biomarkers for early cancer detection and monitoring.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using DNA-encoded antibody libraries (e.g., via Proximity Ligation Assay, PLA) for multiplexed, specific pre-enrichment of target protein panels from complex biofluids will dramatically improve the sensitivity and quantitative accuracy of subsequent ultra-high-resolution analysis using Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) ion mobility-Orbitrap MS.
proposals.method: We will design a panel of DNA-barcoded antibodies targeting a 50-plex protein set implicated in high-grade serous ovarian cancer. Plasma samples will undergo a solution-phase PLA reaction, where target-bound antibody pairs generate unique, amplifiable DNA tags. These DNA tags will be used to capture and isolate the antibody-protein complexes onto streptavidin beads via biotinylated oligonucleotides. After stringent washing, enriched proteins will be eluted, digested, and analyzed using a dual-gated SLIM-ion mobility-Orbitrap platform. This platform will provide ultra-high-resolution separations (mobility and mass) and enable the application of Individual Ion Mass Spectrometry (I2MS) principles for precise charge state determination and quantification.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, highly multiplexed front-end affinity strategy specifically designed for integration with advanced MS platforms. It will demonstrate a pipeline for quantifying dozens of low-abundance plasma proteins at clinically relevant levels, directly addressing the sensitivity gap in proteogenomic studies. The method will provide a template for creating targeted, high-sensitivity assays for any disease-specific protein panel.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct coupling of DNA-encoded, multiplexed affinity enrichment (borrowing from PLA concepts) with the highest-resolution gas-phase separations and measurement capabilities (SLIM, I2MS-informed analysis). This moves beyond single-analyte immunoassays or broad, non-specific depletion, creating a targeted 'molecular filter' optimized for downstream high-resolution MS.

proposals.title: A Dual-Gated SLIM-IMS-Orbitrap Platform with Pre-Fractionation for Ultrasensitive, High-Throughput Detection of Low-Abundance Proteins from Single-Cell and Microdissected Samples
proposals.motivation: The comprehensive characterization of proteomes, especially in clinical biopsies and single-cell analyses, is critically limited by the inability to detect and quantify low-abundance proteins from minute sample amounts. Pre-2026, even advanced mass spectrometry platforms struggle with the dynamic range and sensitivity required to access this biologically crucial 'dark proteome' in high-grade cancers and rare cell populations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a novel, offline high-pH reversed-phase peptide pre-fractionation system with a dual-gated Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM)-Ion Mobility Spectrometry (IMS)-Orbitrap platform will achieve a >10-fold improvement in sensitivity for low-abundance proteins, enabling their reliable quantification from sub-nanogram peptide inputs.
proposals.method: We will develop an integrated workflow where peptides from ~10,000 cells are first separated using an offline 96-well plate-based high-pH reversed-phase fractionator, drastically reducing sample complexity per MS injection. These fractions will be analyzed on a modified dual-gated SLIM-IMS-Orbitrap platform, leveraging ultra-long path SLIM separations (≥20m) for exceptional ion capacity and the dual-gate system for lossless ion packet selection. We will implement Individual Ion Mass Spectrometry (I2MS) algorithms, adapted from FTICR-based charge determination, on the Orbitrap's high-resolution transients to deconvolve and quantify co-eluting, low-abundance species from complex spectra.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, end-to-end analytical platform for ultra-deep proteomics of vanishingly small samples. It will provide a new benchmark for sensitivity in bottom-up proteomics and generate the first proteome maps focusing explicitly on the previously undetectable low-abundance protein cohort in microdissected high-grade serous ovarian cancer tissues.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of an ultra-high-capacity offline pre-fractionation step with the lossless, high-resolution ion manipulation of the dual-gated SLIM-IMS system, specifically tuned and algorithmically supported for the deepest possible mining of low-abundance ions, rather than just high-throughput profiling.

proposals.title: Spatially Resolved Lipidome Mapping of Glioblastoma Subtypes Using High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Imaging Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: Glioblastoma (GBM) is a lethal brain cancer with significant inter- and intra-tumoral heterogeneity, contributing to treatment resistance. Pre-2026 lipidomics studies have identified subtype-associated lipid signatures in bulk tissue, but the spatial distribution of these lipids within the complex tumor microenvironment remains largely unmapped. Understanding this spatial organization is critical for deciphering lipid-mediated signaling, metabolic crosstalk, and identifying region-specific therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct GBM molecular subtypes (proneural, mesenchymal, classical) exhibit unique, spatially organized lipid distributions within tumor regions, peritumoral tissue, and necrotic cores, which correlate with cellular phenotypes and disease drivers.
proposals.method: We will employ high-resolution matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization (MALDI) imaging mass spectrometry coupled to the Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM)-based ion mobility separation platform developed in the Smith lab. This integrated SLIM-Ion Mobility-Orbitrap MS platform will provide ultra-high resolution molecular analysis. Tissue sections from patient-derived GBM xenografts of defined subtypes will be analyzed. Data processing will utilize FTICR-based precise mass measurements and ion mobility collision cross-section values for confident lipid identification, with spatial distributions mapped using specialized imaging software (e.g., SCiLS Lab).
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first spatially resolved lipid atlases for molecular GBM subtypes, directly linking lipid metabolism to tumor histology. It will identify spatially restricted lipid biomarkers and reveal metabolic niches within the tumor microenvironment, offering new insights into GBM biology and potential targets for spatially informed therapies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of ultra-high-resolution SLIM-based ion mobility separations—providing an additional dimension of specificity—with imaging MS to deconvolute the complex lipidome within GBM tissue architecture, moving beyond bulk analysis to achieve spatially precise molecular phenotyping.

proposals.title: Real-Time Single-Cell Mass Spectrometric Phenotyping for the Rational Design of Broad-Spectrum Bacteriocins
proposals.motivation: The rise of multi-drug resistant bacterial infections necessitates novel, narrow-spectrum antimicrobials like bacteriocins, but engineering them for broad-spectrum activity remains slow and empirical. Current screening relies on population-level growth assays, which obscure heterogeneous cellular responses and the precise proteomic mechanisms of action. Integrating high-resolution single-cell analysis with protein engineering could directly link bacteriocin structural variants to their fundamental, cell-resolved effects across diverse targets.
proposals.hypothesis: That real-time, single-cell ESI-MS phenotyping of individual bacterial cells exposed to bacteriocin libraries will reveal conserved proteomic stress signatures, enabling the rational design of broad-spectrum bacteriocins through targeted domain swaps informed by these mechanistic insights.
proposals.method: We will develop a platform coupling a microfluidic cell trap to a nano-electrospray ionization source for single-cell introduction into a high-resolution mass spectrometer, specifically a modified SLIM-enabled ion mobility-Orbitrap platform for ultra-high-resolution analysis. Real-time proteomic phenotyping will use targeted MS/MS to monitor stress response proteins (e.g., RecA, GroEL), membrane damage markers (e.g., released lipids, periplasmic proteins), and effector molecules. Data will be analyzed using algorithms for single-cell trajectory alignment and comparative proteomic signature clustering to identify conserved pathways disrupted by specific bacteriocin domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, high-throughput link between bacteriocin protein structure and single-cell mechanism of action, moving engineering beyond indirect screening. It will produce a novel dataset of single-cell bacterial stress proteomes and deliver a platform for rationally designing broad-spectrum antimicrobial peptides with defined cellular targets.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of real-time, single-cell proteomic phenotyping with bacteriocin engineering is unprecedented, moving from population-level inhibition metrics to mechanistic, cell-resolved design principles. The application of ultra-high-resolution SLIM-Orbitrap MS to monitor dynamic proteomic changes in individual, live bacterial cells represents a significant technical advance.

proposals.title: Enhanced Proteome Depth for Complex Tissues via Concatenated High-pH Reversed-Phase Liquid Chromatography Fractionation Coupled to High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: Deep, reproducible profiling of complex tissue proteomes remains a critical challenge in systems biology and biomarker discovery. While concatenated high-pH reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) fractionation has shown promise for simplifying peptide mixtures from cell lines, its performance on more challenging, protein-rich, and matrix-laden samples like solid tissues or bodily fluids is not well characterized. Extending this strategy to such samples is essential for translating proteogenomic findings, like those in ovarian cancer, into clinically relevant analyses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a concatenated high-pH RPLC fractionation strategy to complex tissue lysates will significantly increase proteome coverage and quantitative reproducibility compared to standard low-pH single-dimension methods, by reducing sample complexity prior to ultra-high-resolution analysis.
proposals.method: We will process human high-grade serous ovarian cancer tissue lysates and matched plasma samples. Peptides will be fractionated using offline high-pH RPLC, with fractions concatenated based on their elution profile to reduce analysis time while maintaining resolution. These fractions will be analyzed using our established dual-gated Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) - Ion Mobility - Orbitrap MS platform. Data will be processed with the FragPipe computational platform, leveraging the MSFragger algorithm for comprehensive identification and the Philosopher toolkit for confident protein inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, optimized sample preparation pipeline for deep proteomic analysis of tissues, directly enhancing the depth of future proteogenomic studies. It will establish the practical limits of detection and quantitative precision for concatenated fractionation on clinically relevant samples, bridging the gap between advanced instrumental platforms and robust sample processing.
proposals.novelty: While high-pH fractionation is known, its concatenated application has not been systematically evaluated for the unique challenges of solid tissue proteomes. Combining this strategy with the latest ultra-high-resolution ion mobility-MS platforms represents a novel integration of front-end separation with back-end analysis to push the boundaries of measurable proteome depth.

proposals.title: An Automated, High-Throughput Platform for Deep Plasma Proteome Analysis of Large Clinical Cohorts Using Parallelized SLIM Fractionation and Ultra-High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Orbitrap Mass Spectrometry
proposals.motivation: The discovery and validation of robust protein biomarkers for diseases like cancer require the deep, reproducible analysis of hundreds of clinical samples, a scale currently bottlenecked by manual, low-throughput sample preparation and lengthy LC-MS/MS cycles. Pre-2026 proteogenomic studies, such as those on ovarian cancer, demonstrate the power of deep profiling but are limited to tens of samples, hindering statistical power and clinical translation. Scaling these workflows is a critical unmet need in translational proteomics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating automated liquid handling for sample preparation with a parallelized, multi-channel Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) separation system coupled to high-field asymmetric waveform ion mobility spectrometry (FAIMS) and an Orbitrap mass spectrometer, we can achieve a >10-fold increase in throughput for deep plasma proteome analysis without sacrificing depth or quantitative accuracy.
proposals.method: We will develop an integrated robotic platform (e.g., Hamilton STAR) for automated protein digestion, cleanup, and TMTpro 16-plex labeling. Labeled peptides will be fractionated using a novel, multiplexed SLIM-based high-resolution ion mobility (HRIM) device operating in parallel channels, replacing serial offline HPLC. Each mobility-separated stream will be analyzed in real-time via FAIMS-filtered, data-independent acquisition (DIA) on an Orbitrap Eclipse Tribrid MS. A cloud-based computational pipeline, leveraging the DIA-NN algorithm, will automate spectral library generation from pooled fractions and provide cohort-scale quantification and statistical analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a complete, automated pipeline for processing hundreds of plasma samples to a depth of >5,000 proteins per sample within a practical timeframe. It will provide the proteomics community with a blueprint and open-source software for scalable cohort analysis. The resulting platform will directly enable statistically powerful biomarker discovery studies in large biobanks.
proposals.novelty: While SLIM and FAIMS have been used for ultra-high-resolution analysis, their integration into a parallelized, automated front-end specifically designed for cohort-scale throughput is novel. This moves beyond demonstrating resolution on single samples to solving the critical throughput bottleneck for population-scale proteomics.

proposals.title: Rapid, Shallow Proteome Profiling via Reduced-Fraction SLIM-IMS-MS and Compressed Sensing Data Analysis
proposals.motivation: Deep, multi-fraction proteomic profiling is time-intensive and limits throughput for clinical and systems biology applications requiring rapid sample triage or longitudinal monitoring. Pre-2026, the challenge has been to maintain sufficient proteome coverage and quantitative accuracy while drastically simplifying the workflow. This proposal addresses the inverse problem: determining the minimal fractionation and data acquisition complexity needed for a defined profiling goal.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically reducing offline fractionation steps and leveraging the ultra-high resolution of SLIM-IMS-Orbitrap platforms for in-line ion separation, coupled with compressed sensing algorithms for spectral demultiplexing, we can achieve a >10x speed increase in sample processing while retaining >70% of the proteome coverage and quantitative precision needed for robust differential expression analysis.
proposals.method: We will develop a method using only 1-3 concatenated high-pH reversed-phase fractions per sample, analyzed on a dual-gated SLIM-IMS-Orbitrap platform. The ultra-high-resolution IMS and MS dimensions will provide the necessary peak capacity to compensate for reduced LC separation. We will implement a novel data analysis pipeline based on the 'Reactions for Charge Determination' principle and compressive sensing algorithms (e.g., orthogonal matching pursuit) to deconvolve complex, co-eluting precursor masses from the reduced-fraction data, effectively reconstructing deeper proteomic information from shallow acquisitions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, streamlined workflow for rapid proteome profiling, making high-quality proteomics more accessible for time-sensitive applications like intraoperative diagnostics or large-scale population screens. It will establish a formal framework for trading depth for speed in experimental design, guided by the analytical performance of advanced ion mobility-mass spectrometry.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the intentional under-sampling of the separation space combined with algorithmic reconstruction, a concept not fully explored in proteomics pre-2026. It uniquely integrates the extreme resolution of SLIM-IMS with signal processing techniques to solve the inverse problem of minimal-input proteome profiling.

proposals.title: Interrogating the Serum Proteome Dark Matter: A High-Resolution SLIM-IMS-FTMS and De Novo Sequencing Strategy to Reveal Unannotated Proteins
proposals.motivation: Current serum proteomics relies on reference databases derived from known genomes and predicted ORFs, which may be incomplete. This creates a fundamental bias, potentially missing novel proteins from unannotated open reading frames (uORFs), alternative splicing, post-translational processing, or microbial co-metabolites. Comprehensively cataloging the serum proteome is critical for discovering next-generation biomarkers for early disease detection and understanding host-pathogen interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant population of novel, unannotated, or microorganism-derived proteins exists in human serum that are systematically missed by standard database-dependent search algorithms in mass spectrometry.
proposals.method: We will employ an ultra-high-resolution platform integrating Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) for high-efficiency, long-path ion mobility separation, coupled to Fourier transform ion cyclotron resonance (FTICR) mass spectrometry for unparalleled mass accuracy. Serum proteins will be fractionated and digested, then analyzed with data-independent acquisition (DIA). Spectra will be processed using a hybrid search strategy: first, against canonical databases (UniProt), then using de novo sequencing algorithms (e.g., PEAKS, Novor) informed by the high-confidence mobility-mass correlation to generate sequence tags. These tags will be searched against six-frame translations of the human genome and non-redundant microbial databases.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel analytical and bioinformatic workflow to move beyond database-limited proteomics. It will produce a first-of-its-kind resource of confidently identified 'dark matter' serum peptides, provide a benchmark for database completeness, and deliver open-source software for hybrid spectral identification. This directly advances the goal of a complete human proteome map.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic application of ultra-high-resolution SLIM-IMS-FTMS, which provides pristine separations and clean spectra ideal for de novo sequencing, with a targeted bioinformatic hunt for sequences absent from standard databases. This moves beyond simply identifying known proteins to actively discovering the unknown proteomic constituents of serum.

proposals.title: A Multi-Modal Depletion Strategy Coupled with SLIM-IMS-High-Field Orbitrap MS for Deep Plasma Proteome Profiling
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in mass spectrometry sensitivity, the detection of low-abundance plasma biomarkers remains hindered by the dynamic range problem, where a few proteins like albumin and immunoglobulins dominate the signal. Current immunoaffinity depletion columns are limited in scope and can co-deplete proteins of interest. A more comprehensive and specific depletion method is critical for advancing proteogenomic studies of diseases like ovarian cancer, where deep profiling could reveal novel therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequential, multi-modal depletion strategy combining high-specificity immunoaffinity with orthogonal physicochemical separations will remove a broader spectrum of high-abundance proteins with minimal non-specific binding, thereby significantly increasing the depth of coverage for the low-abundance plasma proteome.
proposals.method: We will develop a sequential sample preparation workflow: first, a refined immunoaffinity column targeting an expanded panel of 14 high-abundance proteins; second, a peptide-level library-scale depletion using high-pH reverse-phase fractionation to isolate and remove peptides originating from the top 50 most abundant plasma proteins, guided by prior FTICR measurements. The efficacy will be evaluated using our established platform integrating Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) for ultra-high-resolution ion mobility separation, polarized ion gating for selectivity, and high-field Orbitrap mass spectrometry for analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robust, standardized protocol for deep plasma proteome analysis, pushing detection limits for low-abundance proteins. It will directly enhance integrated proteogenomic characterizations by providing cleaner input for downstream MS analysis, enabling the discovery of previously obscured protein variants and post-translational modifications relevant to cancer biology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic combination of protein- and peptide-level depletion informed by ultra-high-resolution historical FTICR data, and the direct performance validation using a cutting-edge SLIM-IMS-Orbitrap platform, moving beyond simple column improvements to a systems-level preparation strategy.

proposals.title: A SILAC-Inspired, High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Mass Spectrometry Platform for Quantitative Serum Proteome Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Serum proteomics is critical for biomarker discovery but is hampered by high dynamic range and biological variability, making precise quantification challenging. Pre-2026, SILAC is a gold standard for in vitro quantitative proteomics but is fundamentally incompatible with direct human serum analysis. There is a pressing need to adapt the core principles of metabolic isotopic labeling to create a robust internal standard method for complex biofluids.
proposals.hypothesis: That a workflow combining in-vitro metabolic engineering of a human cell line with ultra-high-resolution ion mobility-Orbitrap MS can generate a multiplexed, isobaric-tagged 'reference proteome' that, when spiked into serum, enables accurate, multiplexed quantification of hundreds of low-abundance proteins and their isoforms.
proposals.method: We will metabolically engineer a HEK293 cell line to express a 'super-SILAC' proteome using 13C6,15N4-arginine and 13C6,15N2-lysine. This heavy-labeled proteome will be extracted, digested, and labeled with isobaric tandem mass tags (TMTpro 16-plex). Aliquots will be spiked into individual serum samples as an internal standard. Analysis will use the described dual-gated SLIM-Orbitrap platform, leveraging its ultra-high ion mobility resolution to separate isobaric tags and isotopologues prior to high-resolution FT-MS analysis. Data will be processed with a custom algorithm correlating the mobility-aligned heavy reference peaks with native serum peptide signals for quantification.
proposals.contribution: This will establish a novel quantitative framework for serum proteomics that decouples quantification from ionization variability, a major limitation of label-free methods. It will enable the direct measurement of proteome turnover rates in longitudinal serum studies by tracking the incorporation of stable isotopes from administered tracers against the engineered internal standard.
proposals.novelty: The innovation lies not in applying SILAC to serum directly, but in using a metabolically engineered, isobarically tagged proteome as a universal, multiplexed internal standard, analyzed via a SLIM-IMS platform to resolve quantification interferences that plague traditional MS2-based isobaric tag methods.

proposals.title: De Novo Peptide Assembly via Genome-Inspired Algorithms: A Database-Free Approach for High-Resolution Mass Spectrometry Data
proposals.motivation: Current proteomics relies heavily on reference protein databases, limiting the discovery of novel peptides, splice variants, and mutations, especially in cancer and microbiome studies. High-resolution instruments like SLIM-based ion mobility-Orbitrap and FTICR systems generate immense, complex datasets, but the lack of a de novo assembly framework analogous to genome sequencing leaves a vast amount of proteomic 'dark matter' uncharacterized. This gap hinders the full realization of proteogenomic and single-cell analyses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that algorithms and data structures from de novo genome assembly (e.g., overlap-layout-consensus, de Bruijn graphs) can be adapted to assemble peptide sequences directly from ultra-high-resolution tandem mass spectra, enabling the discovery of sequences absent from any reference database.
proposals.method: We will adapt the de Bruijn graph paradigm, treating high-confidence peptide-spectrum matches (PSMs) from our dual-gated SLIM-IM-Orbitrap platform as 'reads'. Spectral deconvolution and charge determination techniques from FTICR charge determination mass spectrometry will generate accurate mass 'k-mers'. An overlap-layout-consensus assembler, inspired by Celera Assembler, will be developed to merge these k-mers into longer peptide contigs, using ion mobility-derived collision cross-sections as an orthogonal constraint for graph path validation. The system will be tested on data from high-grade serous ovarian cancer cell lines.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first computational framework for de novo peptide assembly, moving beyond single-spectrum de novo sequencing. It will provide a new tool for discovering unannotated proteins, novel biomarkers, and microbial peptides in complex samples. The method will directly enhance the utility of ultra-high-resolution ion mobility-mass spectrometry platforms for exploratory biology.
proposals.novelty: While de novo sequencing from single spectra exists, applying scalable genome assembly techniques to assemble a 'peptidome' from millions of spectra is a novel conceptual leap. The integration of ion mobility-derived physicochemical properties as assembly constraints is a unique innovation not present in genomic or existing proteomic algorithms.

proposals.title: Nanoparticle-Enabled Deep Proteomic Profiling of Serum via High-Resolution Ion Mobility-Mass Spectrometry for Early Cancer Biomarker Discovery
proposals.motivation: The detection of low-abundance serum proteins is a critical unmet need in clinical proteomics, particularly for the early discovery of cancer biomarkers. Current mass spectrometry (MS) workflows are limited by the immense dynamic range of serum, which obscures trace proteins of potential diagnostic value. Pre-2026, while high-resolution platforms like SLIM-based ion mobility-Orbitrap MS exist, they still require effective upstream enrichment to realize their full analytical potential for deep proteome coverage.
proposals.hypothesis: Functionalized magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) with tailored surface chemistries can efficiently capture and concentrate low-abundance proteins from large-volume serum samples, enabling their subsequent detection and identification by ultra-high-resolution ion mobility-mass spectrometry where they were previously undetectable.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and functionalize superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles with a library of chemical baits (e.g., mixed-mode ligands, hydrophilic polymers, immobilized metal chelates) to target diverse protein properties. These MNPs will be deployed to process multi-mL volumes of serum from high-grade serous ovarian cancer patients and controls. The enriched proteome will be analyzed using our established dual-gated Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) coupled to an Orbitrap mass spectrometer. Data-independent acquisition (DIA) will be employed, and the resulting ultra-high-resolution ion mobility and mass spectra will be processed using a modified version of the MSFragger algorithm for comprehensive spectral matching and protein inference.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, scalable pipeline for deep serum proteomics, directly coupling nanotechnology-based enrichment with the latest high-resolution ion mobility-MS. It will generate a uniquely detailed proteomic map of ovarian cancer serum, identifying candidate low-abundance biomarkers previously masked by high-abundance proteins. The methodology will be a template for extending the detection limits of clinical proteomics platforms.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a systematic, multi-chemistry nanoparticle capture platform with the combined ultra-high resolution of SLIM-ion mobility and Orbitrap MS for serum analysis is novel. This approach moves beyond single-antibody enrichment to a broad, physicochemical fractionation strategy specifically designed to feed the most advanced pre-2026 MS instrumentation.

proposals.title: A Minimal Proteome Signature for Ovarian Cancer Detection via SLIM-Dual-Gated SLIM-IM-Orbitrap MS and Sparse Machine Learning
proposals.motivation: High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSOC) remains a lethal malignancy often diagnosed at late stages. While proteogenomic studies, including Smith's prior work, have cataloged thousands of dysregulated proteins, translating these discoveries into clinically viable, early detection tests requires identifying a minimal, robust set of biomarkers. Current mass spectrometry (MS) panels are often empirically derived and lack a principled framework for selecting the smallest, most informative protein subset from ultra-high-resolution discovery data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sparse, interpretable machine learning model, applied to ultra-high-resolution ion mobility-mass spectrometry proteomic data, can identify a minimal subset (<10) of circulating proteins that accurately discriminates HGSOC from benign conditions with performance rivaling larger panels.
proposals.method: We will leverage our established platform integrating Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) with dual-gated ion routing, high-field asymmetric waveform ion mobility spectrometry (FAIMS), and Orbitrap MS for deep, reproducible plasma proteome profiling of HGSOC and control cohorts. From the resulting high-mobility-resolution datasets, feature selection will be performed using L1-regularized (LASSO) logistic regression and stability selection algorithms to identify the most consistently predictive proteins. Model performance will be validated on a held-out cohort using targeted parallel reaction monitoring (PRM) assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a rigorously defined, ultra-low-plex proteomic signature for HGSOC detection, directly addressing the translational gap in proteogenomics. It will establish a generalizable computational-experimental pipeline for distilling complex MS discovery data into minimal diagnostic panels, moving the field toward cost-effective, high-specificity clinical assays.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct application of sparse modeling to the uniquely high-resolution separations from a SLIM-dual-gated-IM-MS platform, explicitly solving the 'inverse problem' of minimal marker identification from maximal data, a task distinct from standard differential expression analysis.

proposals.title: A Dual-Gated SLIM-IMS-FTICR-MS Platform with Pre-Fractionation for Ultrasensitive, High-Dynamic-Range Plasma Proteome Profiling Down to pg/mL
proposals.motivation: Current mass spectrometry-based plasma proteomics is fundamentally limited by a dynamic range challenge, with high-abundance proteins obscuring the detection of low-abundance biomarkers, cytokines, and signaling proteins present at sub-ng/mL concentrations. This pre-2026 bottleneck prevents the discovery of crucial early-disease biomarkers and the study of fundamental intercellular communication. Advancing detection sensitivity by three orders of magnitude, from ng/mL to pg/mL, is essential for transformative applications in early cancer detection and systems biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating high-efficiency, multi-dimensional pre-fractionation with ultra-high-resolution ion mobility separations and Fourier-transform ion cyclotron resonance (FTICR) mass analysis will systematically deconvolute the plasma proteome's dynamic range, enabling the consistent identification and quantification of proteins at pg/mL concentrations.
proposals.method: We will develop a platform coupling offline, low-flow-rate capillary electrophoresis (CE) and affinity depletion for deep pre-fractionation to reduce sample complexity prior to MS. Ionization efficiency will be maximized using nano-electrospray emitters. Ions will be introduced into a dual-gated Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM) module for ultra-long-path, high-resolution ion mobility separations, effectively resolving isobaric species. Finally, ions will be analyzed by a high-field FTICR mass spectrometer, leveraging its ultra-high mass resolution and mass accuracy, as demonstrated in our prior charge determination work, to definitively identify low-abundance species.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated technological pipeline capable of profiling the plasma proteome with unprecedented depth, reliably quantifying proteins across a >10^12 concentration range. It will provide a new open-source data resource of low-abundance plasma proteins and establish a benchmark for ultrasensitive biomarker discovery. The platform will be a critical tool for probing previously inaccessible low-abundance proteomic networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific integration of CE and SLIM-IMS for dynamic range compression prior to FTICR analysis, a combination not yet fully explored pre-2026. This moves beyond simply improving instrument sensitivity to a systems-level approach that attacks the dynamic range problem at multiple points in the analytical workflow.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput, High-Fidelity CE-SLIM-Orbitrap Platform for Ultrasensitive Proteoform Analysis in Single Cells
proposals.motivation: Capillary electrophoresis (CE) offers superior separation efficiency for complex biological mixtures, but traditional UV detection lacks definitive identification power. In proteomics, especially for single-cell analysis and characterizing proteoforms (e.g., from alternative splicing or post-translational modifications), correlating CE migration time with molecular identity is insufficient. A direct, high-sensitivity interface between CE and ultra-high-resolution mass spectrometry (MS) is critically needed to unlock CE's full potential for deep molecular profiling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that interfacing a nanoscale CE system directly with a Structures for Lossless Ion Manipulations (SLIM)-based ion mobility spectrometer coupled to an Orbitrap mass analyzer will enable the definitive identification and characterization of low-abundance proteoforms separated by CE with unprecedented sensitivity and resolution.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel nano-electrospray ionization (nano-ESI) interface to directly couple a commercial CE system to a dual-gated SLIM-Orbitrap platform. The SLIM module will provide additional, orthogonal separation via high-resolution ion mobility (IMS) after CE, filtering chemical noise and isobaric interferences. We will employ established algorithms for IMS-MS feature detection (e.g., Decon2LS, MaxQuant) and develop new data integration routines to align CE migration time, IMS drift time, and accurate mass-to-charge (m/z) from the Orbitrap. The platform will be validated using complex protein digests and intact protein standards before application to single-cell lysates.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, high-sensitivity CE-MS platform that merges the superior separation of CE with the definitive identification power of ultra-high-resolution MS/MS and IMS. It will provide a new tool for the proteogenomic community to deeply characterize proteoforms, directly building on prior integrated characterization work. The method will push the limits of detection for single-cell proteomics, enabling the study of protein isoforms and modifications previously inaccessible.
proposals.novelty: While CE-MS interfaces exist, the integration of CE with the ultra-high-resolution ion mobility separation of SLIM and the mass accuracy of an Orbitrap for intact protein and proteoform analysis is novel. This combines Smith's expertise in SLIM, high-field asymmetric waveform ion mobility spectrometry (FAIMS), and FT-based mass analysis into a unique workflow that adds a critical separation dimension prior to MS analysis.

ideas.description: This research direction fuses the engineering of broad-spectrum bacteriocins with miniaturized single-cell ESI-MS to create a high-resolution discovery engine. The miniaturized MS will be used to analyze the real-time proteomic changes—such as stress response signatures, membrane damage markers, and intracellular effector release—in individual bacterial cells from different species upon exposure to bacteriocin libraries. This single-cell phenotypic data directly informs which protein engineering strategies (e.g., receptor-binding domain swaps, pore-forming module combinations) most effectively disrupt conserved pathways across diverse targets. Consequently, the platform enables the rational design of broad-spectrum bacteriocins by linking structural variants to their fundamental, cell-resolved mechanisms of action, accelerating development beyond traditional, slow, and indirect screening methods.

ideas.description: This research direction fuses protein engineering for ESI-MS with spatially resolved tissue analysis by developing a co-optimized system of 'designer tags' and 'cleavage probes'. Protein sequences or fusion tags are engineered to contain labile linkers and ionization-enhancing motifs that are specifically targeted by reactive reagents released from a spatially directed ESI-MS probe upon tissue contact. This allows for the selective liberation and immediate ionization of tagged proteins of interest directly from a defined tissue location. The synergy enables multiplexed, on-demand mapping of low-abundance proteins, such as signaling complexes or post-translationally modified variants, within native tissue architecture, bypassing the need for extraction, separation, or broad-spectrum digestion.

ideas.title: Adapting 'Click Chemistry' for Spatially Programmed Tissue Ligation and ESI-MS Analysis
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from chemical biology's solution for selective biomolecule tagging in complex environments. The analogous field is Chemical Biology/Bioorthogonal Chemistry, where the problem of specific labeling was solved using bioorthogonal 'click chemistry' reactions to attach probes to target molecules in living systems. We adapt this 'tag-and-capture' paradigm for direct tissue analysis. The research involves developing a library of ESI-MS-compatible, 'clickable' chemical probes designed to covalently bind specific protein classes (e.g., those with active-site nucleophiles or specific post-translational modifications). These probes are applied to a tissue section in a defined spatial pattern using a stamping or arraying technique. Following the spatially programmed ligation, the entire tissue surface is analyzed by desorption ESI-MS. The mass spectrometer detects the cleaved probe-protein conjugate ions; the probe's mass acts as a reporter tag identifying the bound protein, while the spatial origin of the signal is inferred from the pre-defined application pattern of that specific probe. This approach uses chemical scaling (the programmable probe application) to manage biological complexity and enable multiplexed, spatially resolved protein analysis directly from tissue.

ideas.title: Adapting DNA Microarray Parallelization for High-Throughput Clinical ESI-MS
ideas.description: This idea adapts the spatial multiplexing and parallelization solution from genomics—specifically DNA microarray technology—to the challenge of scaling up ESI-MS for high-throughput clinical protein screening. In genomics, the analogous problem of analyzing thousands of gene expression samples was solved by creating microarrays: ordered grids of DNA probes on a solid substrate that allowed a single experiment to query thousands of targets in parallel via automated scanning. We adapt this by proposing the development of a 'MS Array Chip.' This chip would contain a high-density array of individually addressable nano-ESI emitters arranged in a precise grid. Each emitter position could be dedicated to a different clinical sample (e.g., from a microtiter plate via automated liquid handling) or functionalized with specific capture agents for targeted proteomics. The chip would be integrated with an automated XY stage and a single mass spectrometer. The system would rapidly sequence through the array, acquiring data from each emitter position with minimal dead time, thereby automating and parallelizing sample introduction and analysis. This transforms ESI-MS from a primarily serial technique into a high-throughput, parallel analytical platform suitable for large-scale clinical screening campaigns.

ideas.title: Protein Engineering for ESI-MS via Antenna Design Principles
ideas.description: This idea applies the solution from Radio Frequency Antenna Engineering to the original protein design problem for ESI-MS. In RF, antennas are designed with specific geometries to efficiently couple energy between a circuit and free space. The analogous solution is adapted by treating the electrospray ionization process as the 'transmitter' and the mass spectrometer as the 'receiver.' The research would involve computationally modeling the electrostatic and hydrodynamic environment during ESI to design protein sequences or modular tags. These designs would function as ionization antennas, optimizing surface charge distribution and local structure to maximize the efficient and reproducible generation of gas-phase ions, thereby dramatically improving detection limits and quantitative accuracy in ESI-MS analyses.

ideas.title: Ion Mobility-Guided Engineering of Broad-Spectrum Bacteriocins
ideas.description: This research direction couples ion mobility spectrometry (IMS) with ESI-MS to analyze the gas-phase conformations of bacteriocins and their complexes with receptors from different bacterial species. By resolving distinct conformational families, the method identifies which structural features correlate with binding to non-cognate receptors, challenging the species-specificity paradigm. This structural insight directly informs protein engineering, enabling the rational design of bacteriocin variants that maintain stable, binding-competent conformations across multiple target receptors. The synergy creates a closed-loop platform where IMS-MS provides rapid conformational feedback to guide the iterative development of engineered, broad-spectrum antimicrobial peptides.

ideas.title: Adapting Photonic Crystal Cavities for Enhanced ESI-MS Ion Capture and Transmission
ideas.description: This idea applies the photonic solution for detecting weak light signals to the problem of low-concentration protein analysis in ESI-MS. In photonics, high-finesse optical cavities trap and recirculate light to amplify weak signals. Adapted to mass spectrometry, the research would involve developing novel ESI-MS interfaces and ion optics featuring micro-fabricated 'ion cavities'—structures using precisely controlled electromagnetic fields to resonantly trap and recirculate ions of a target mass range. This repeated recirculation increases the effective ion path length and the probability of transmission to the detector, analogous to light buildup in an optical cavity. The goal is to significantly improve ion transmission efficiency and signal-to-noise ratio for trace proteins, enabling analysis at previously undetectable concentrations.

ideas.title: Coupled Buffer-Source Engineering for Native ESI-MS via Dynamic Desolvation Control
ideas.description: This project addresses the fundamental incompatibility between aqueous buffers and efficient electrospray by co-optimizing the solution chemistry and the physical ionization process. Instead of seeking a single buffer replacement, we design a dynamically tunable two-component system: a primary aqueous, native-preserving buffer is mixed in-line with a minimal, metered amount of a volatile desolvation promoter immediately before electrospray. Simultaneously, a feedback-controlled ESI source uses droplet imaging diagnostics to adjust temperature, gas flow, and voltage in real-time, creating tailored conditions for each stage of droplet evolution. This integrated approach aims to achieve the necessary desolvation for sensitive detection while maintaining the protein in its native state throughout the ionization pathway.

ideas.title: Controlled Collisional Activation in ESI-MS to Map Protein Energy Landscapes
ideas.description: This idea inverts the standard assumption of gentle ESI by deliberately using harsher conditions, but does so in a controlled, analogical manner inspired by chemical engineering. The analogous field is chemical reaction engineering, where 'severity-programmed' reactors map complex reaction networks by progressively increasing thermal severity. The adapted solution is to implement a controlled, stepwise ramp of 'ionization severity' within an ESI source by systematically increasing parameters like nozzle-skimmer voltage, source temperature, or solvent aggressiveness. By monitoring the resulting mass spectral changes at each severity step, one can construct a detailed unfolding profile for a protein. This approach probes unfolding pathways and stability by revealing the sequence of domain dissociations and the population of meta-stable intermediates, much like a reaction network map, providing a novel dynamic perspective on protein energy landscapes under non-native conditions.

ideas.title: Adapt Cryo-EM Sample Preparation for Native ESI-MS of Megadalton Complexes
ideas.description: The challenge of studying very large, non-covalent protein complexes via ESI-MS is analogous to the historical challenge of preparing intact, hydrated specimens for cryo-EM imaging. In cryo-EM, specialized support grids and rapid vitrification were key to preserving native structures. This idea adapts that solution by developing novel ESI-MS sample introduction systems. By functionalizing emitters with cryo-EM-inspired support films (e.g., graphene oxide) and optimizing buffer-to-gas phase transition conditions to mimic vitrification's speed and gentleness, the goal is to extend the practical mass and stability limits of native MS. This would enable the direct analysis of oligomeric states and subunit interactions in megadalton complexes like intact ribosomes, viral capsids, or large molecular machines, which are currently at or beyond the fringe of ESI-MS capabilities.

ideas.title: Energy-Resolved Unfolding Landscapes via Decoupled Perturbation and Ionization
ideas.description: This approach systematically probes protein stability by applying controlled, discrete perturbations prior to gentle electrospray ionization. A modular source design separates the unfolding stimulus—such as precise thermal heating, collisional activation in an intermediate pressure region, or solution-phase destabilization—from the ionization process itself. By titrating the intensity of each perturbation type independently while using standardized soft ESI to generate ions, the method creates multidimensional stability maps. This allows the unambiguous assignment of unfolding pathways to specific biophysical forces, transforming a chaotic harsh-ESI experiment into a quantitative tool for studying protein energy landscapes.

ideas.title: Adaptive Optics for Electrospray: Using Spectral Feedback to Optimize Ion Source Parameters
ideas.description: This idea adapts the closed-loop feedback solution from adaptive optics in astronomy to the problem of optimizing an electrospray ionization (ESI) source for mass spectrometry. In adaptive optics, a wavefront sensor provides real-time measurements of optical distortion, which a control system uses to dynamically reshape a mirror and correct the image. The analogous solution here is to treat the mass spectrometer's acquired spectrum as the sensor input. A control algorithm would continuously analyze spectral features (e.g., total ion current, target ion intensity, chemical noise levels) and use this feedback to automatically and iteratively tune ESI voltage, nebulizing gas pressure, source geometry positioning, and other parameters. This creates a 'self-optimizing' ion source that adapts to changing sample matrices and instrument conditions, aiming to consistently produce the highest quality signal without manual intervention.

ideas.title: Integrated CE-MS Interface with Active Flow-Gating for Stable Nano-Electrospray
ideas.description: This system redesigns the CE-ESI interface to directly solve the critical flow rate mismatch. It incorporates a flow-gating or zero-dead-volume junction that introduces a minimal, precisely metered makeup flow, just sufficient to stabilize the electrospray without diluting the CE-separated bands. This interface maintains the electrical continuity for CE separation while providing independent control for ESI voltage, ensuring robust spray formation. The result is a robust, practical platform that preserves the high-resolution separation power of CE for sensitive and quantitative MS detection.

ideas.title: Context-Activated, Broad-Spectrum Bacteriocins with Ecological Safeguards
ideas.description: This project engineers broad-spectrum bacteriocin systems that are not constitutively active but are instead controlled by environmentally responsive genetic circuits. The bacteriocin's lethal activity is coupled to the expression of a neutralizing immunity protein, with the expression of this safeguard triggered by a specific signal present only at the intended site of action, such as a pathogen-associated metabolite or low pH. This design maintains the therapeutic power of a broad-spectrum agent while preventing collateral damage to off-target, beneficial microbial communities. The system's efficacy and ecological safety will be validated using in vitro gut microbiome models and in vivo infection models.

ideas.title: Engineering Broad-Spectrum Bacteriocins via Modular Receptor-Binding Domains
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that bacteriocins must be species-specific by applying a solution from virology. In viral vector engineering, a common method to broaden tropism is to modify the viral surface proteins responsible for host cell recognition. Analogously, bacteriocins can be viewed as modular proteins. The research would involve structurally and functionally separating the bacteriocin's species-specific receptor-binding domain from its conserved pore-forming or nuclease domain. By engineering a library of receptor-targeting modules that bind to highly conserved, essential bacterial surface structures (such as lipid II or core peptidoglycan components), and recombining them with the lethal domain, we can create synthetic, broad-spectrum bacteriocin variants. This modular engineering approach could yield novel antimicrobials effective against a wide range of pathogenic bacteria, moving beyond narrow, natural specificity.

ideas.title: Adapting Lab-on-a-Chip Environmental Monitoring to Model Biofilm Microenvironments
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from environmental science, where microfluidic devices are used to model complex natural microenvironments like soil pores. The analogous solution involves creating lab-on-a-chip systems that establish precise chemical gradients and flow conditions to study microbial processes. Adapted to the biofilm infection context, the research would employ microfluidic devices engineered to mimic the specific chemical (nutrient, pH, antibiotic) and physical (flow, shear) gradients of an in vivo infection site. This platform would enable the cultivation of realistic, heterogeneous biofilms for testing how these dynamic microenvironmental conditions affect bacteriocin production, diffusion, and antibacterial activity, providing a more physiologically relevant model for therapeutic development.

ideas.title: Fouling-resistant microfluidic ESI platform with integrated active clearance for robust bioanalysis
ideas.description: This system redesigns the microfluidic-ESI interface to directly combat clogging and fouling, the primary failure mode for handling real-world samples. It incorporates active, on-chip clearance mechanisms such as piezoelectric actuators for acoustic disruption of aggregates and electrowetting zones to dislodge adhered material. The chip architecture features modular, user-replaceable nanofilter cartridges for pre-emitter particulate removal and utilizes inert, fluorinated polymer coatings to minimize protein adsorption. This integrated approach ensures stable, long-term operation and reproducible ion signal by maintaining pristine fluidic pathways and emitter integrity for complex biological matrices.

ideas.title: Host immune context as a modulator of bacteriocin efficacy in chronic infections
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that chronic infections are solely bacterial problems by using an analogy from oncology. In cancer therapy, the success of treatments was found to depend critically on the host's tumor microenvironment and immune state, leading to immunomodulatory solutions. Adapting this, the research idea posits that the efficacy of bacteriocin therapy for chronic bacterial infections is co-determined by the host immune response. The solution is to shift from a pathogen-centric view to a host-pathogen-context view: characterize the immune landscape of the chronic infection site and design therapeutic regimens that combine bacteriocins with agents (e.g., immunomodulators, anti-inflammatory drugs, or vaccines) that shift the immune context to a state permissive for bacteriocin-mediated clearance and durable resolution of infection.

ideas.title: Electrohydrodynamic Bacteriocin Aerosolization for Targeted Deposition in Pan-Resistant Biofilms
ideas.description: This research direction combines the assessment of bacteriocin activity against pan-resistant pathogens with the precise control of electrospray ionization (ESI) droplet dynamics. The core objective is to engineer an electrohydrodynamic aerosolization system that generates optimally sized and charged droplets containing bacteriocins or bacteriocin-loaded nanoparticles. By modeling and controlling the droplet's evaporation, charge, and impact dynamics, the system can be tuned to overcome the hydrophilic and electrostatic barriers of the biofilm matrix, ensuring targeted deposition of the antimicrobial agent directly onto or into the bacterial clusters. This allows for the direct evaluation of bacteriocin potency in a more clinically relevant biofilm context while simultaneously developing a novel, physics-driven delivery modality for next-generation antimicrobials.

ideas.title: Enhance bacteriocin biofilm penetration using enzyme-powered nanomotors
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from cancer nanomedicine, where enzyme-powered nanomotors are used to enhance drug penetration into solid tumors, to the challenge of delivering bacteriocins into biofilms. The analogous solution involves nanoparticles that use catalytic enzymes for both propulsion and tissue disruption. The adapted research idea is to develop bacteriocin-loaded, enzyme-powered nanomotors. These nanomotors would be functionalized with biofilm-disrupting enzymes (e.g., DNase, dispersin B) that also catalyze a reaction with a biofilm fuel source (e.g., glucose, lactate) to generate thrust. This active propulsion, combined with the localized enzymatic degradation of the biofilm matrix, would enable the nanomotors to actively navigate and penetrate the biofilm's depths, delivering bacteriocins directly to target bacteria for a more effective antimicrobial treatment.
